issue_title: Switch docs to Python 3 syntax by default
issue_body:
The docs currently use `sigtools.modifiers` decorators to augment parameters with annotations or to make them keyword-only. I'm suspecting this distracts users from the examples' main points.

Given:

* The above,
* That new python users are increasingly being directed to Python 3,
* That most major packages work on it,
* That Debian and Ubuntu both intend to remove Python 2 from their default installation,

I suggest adjusting the examples so they make use of keyword-only parameters and annotations, and using "note" sections destined to users stuck with Python 2 or trying to keep compatibility with it.
Limit outgoing bytes in mocked HTTP responses.

issue_title: There is no copyright notice in LICENSE
issue_body:
The license in LICENSE says:

> **The above copyright notice** and this permission notice shall be included in
> all copies or substantial portions of the Software.

Yet there is no copyright notice at all, thus technically, nobody can follow the license terms. If you want no copyright, consider Public domain or CC0. If you want MIT, please add the copyright notice. It can be:

> Copyright (c) 2013-2018 pew contributors

I have a few async threads that I want to die when I kill my application, instead of waiting the ~40 or so seconds on a condition that will never be set. It would be great if using just the decorator I could mark these as daemon instead of having to reboot the kernel and kill them manually.

Additionally, the `daemon` property of these async threads don't seem to be used - they're not passed to the underlying threading.Thread.

issue_title: cuppa pulls wrong git branch if default branch is not "master"
issue_body:
We have a git repo which is used as a location dependency in cuppa
The default branch of the repo is not "master", it's "develop"
The branch name is not specified in the location dependency in the SConstruct
cuppa correctly clones the repo with the default branch (develop) in the initial build 
In subsequent builds, cuppa pulls from the master branch and the build fails.

SConstruct:
```
    default_options = {
      'Config-location'  : 'git+http://my.server/Core-services/Core.git',
      'Config-include'   : 'Config/include',
    },
  dependencies = [
      cuppa.location_dependency( 'Config' ),
  ],
```

First build, it clones and checkout the "develop" branch (correct)
```
cuppa: location: __init__:430 [debug] Using [git+http://my.server/Core-services/Core.git] at [develop rev. f4309f6] stored in [/home/dmsdev/repo/Darwin/_cuppa/git_http_my.server__Core-services_Core.git]
```
Subsequent builds, it pulls from origin/master and the build breaks
```
cuppa: location: get_local_directory:289 [debug] Updating [git+http://my.server/Core-services/Core.git] in [/home/dmsdev/repo/Darwin/_cuppa/git_http_my.server__Core-services_Core.git/] on ['origin/master'] at [develop rev. f4309f6]
```
i think the bug is in location.py:
```
    def get_rev_options( self, vc_type, vcs_backend ):
        url, rev = vcs_backend.get_url_rev()
        if vc_type == 'git':
            if rev:
                return [rev]
            else:
                return ['origin/master'] /////////////// this is where the problem comes from
```
I'm not sure exactly how to fix the code, but it needs to to get the branch name by looking at the local repo (or something similar...) instead of assuming it's master branch.

Workaround is to explicitly specify the branch name in the location field, e.g.
```
      'Config-location'  : 'git+http://ldnpbldjen01.eur.ad.tullib.com/Core-services/Core.git@develop',
```

issue_title: Deprecated C++ features actually removed from compiler
issue_body:
Dlib uses some very old C++ features that are deprecated, like auto_ptr. Usually this led to compiler warnings that these features should not be used and this was 'fixed' just by quelling them:
https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/smart_pointers/shared_ptr.h#L13
Now under some scenarios these deprecated features became unavailable at all. For example, in Microsoft Studio 2017 when compiling with -std:c++latest option auto_ptr is simply non-existent identifier. 
This means DLib's header files can't be used in projects that use C++17 libraries/code unless it is compiled as a separate module wrapped into a compatible interface. Though the C++17 is not a standard yet, and neither it is known to me if Microsoft plans to get rid of auto_ptr for good, I think it still makes since to let people at least prototype in what might become C++17. I'd suggest that any features marked as deprecated in the standard should be used only when required (e.g. if an old compiler is detected) and not by default.
To solve this problem for at least in my own case I would like to replace the dlib's shared_ptr with stdlib's one. Is it safe to assume that I can just get away with replacing the implementation with standard one, or are there any behavioural difference between the two?

issue_title: Silent fallback to crypted not good solution
issue_body:
I run Ubuntu Karmic with the default Gnome desktop. Using pip I installed keyring. Even though I have gnome-keyring and the Python bindings installed, because I did not have the libdbus-1-dev and libgnome-keyring-dev packages installed, keyring silently and with no warning decided to install without the Gnome backend and fallback to crypted file in my homedir instead. This sucks.

I think instead the environment check (i.e. what keyring.backend.GnomeKeyring._recommend() does) should be run, and if that returns True but pkg-config fails, I should at least get a big fat warning telling me which dev libs I need to install.

issue_title: Multiple flowcharts with the same name.
issue_body:
Hi,

I have a py file with two flow charts with the same name, This is because
1) I want to process multiple set of rules on all the events hence two flow charts
2) I want to post only on a single endpoint hence flow charts names are the same.

My code looks something like below(with few more stages):



     with flowchart('test'):
        # initial stage 'input' has two conditions
        with stage('input'):
            @run
            def category_human(c):
                print("In stage input")
            to('human').when_all(m.category.matches('.*human.*'))
    
        with stage('human'):
            @run
            def upgrade_complete(c):
                print("In stage human")
    
        @when_start
        def start(host):
            pass
    
    with flowchart('test'):
        with stage('enter'):
            @run
            def bird(c):
                print("In stage enter")
            to('bird').when_all(m.category.matches('.*bird.*'))
    
        with stage('bird'):
            @run
            def category_bird(c):
                print("In stage bird")
    
        @when_start
        def start(host):
            pass
    
    run_all()



And I post from different py file like below


    import requests
     
    rules_url = "http://localhost:5000/test/events"
    headers = {'content-type': 'application/json', 'charset': 'utf-8'}
    event = '{"category": "bird"}'
    r = requests.post(rules_url, data=event, headers=headers)
    event = '{"message": "human"}'
    r = requests.post(rules_url, data=event, headers=headers


However, the output is only, 


    In stage enter
    In stage bird


And

    event = '{"message": "human"}'
 
is not processed at all.
Is there a way I can handle this, so that the output is,


    In stage enter
    In stage bird
    In stage input
    In stage human

issue_title: Query - Is it possible to create a rule between 2 objects from posted JSON ?
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks for building a rules library that can integrate easily into any of the business systems. We are considering using durable_rules engine to solve some of the use cases that occur in our production system. 

One of such cases is where we would like to define a rule the following way : 

from durable.lang import *
with ruleset('test'):
    @when_all(m.paragraph.imatches(m.word))
    def say_hello(c):
        print ('Hello {0}'.format(c.m.paragraph))

    @when_start
    def start(host):
        host.post('test', { 'word': 'World', 'subject':'Hello world via rules' })

run_all()

I tried testing this and received an error : 2 - Unexpected type (uncommon) 

Is it possible to create such rule ? Or how do we go about solving it alternatively with what's already built ?


Thanks.

issue_title: add_command, change_command is inconsistent across widgets
issue_body:
Slider has add_command(command)
PushButton has a change_command(command, args=[])

All widgets which have a `command` should allow the command to be changed/added.

I would propose renaming it to `update_command`, this works if you are adding and changing.

I just stumbled upon your repository while searching for some xpinyin latex stuff.
From you examples I don't see any tones on the pinyin syllables. Does the program not get them? This would reduce usefulness quite a bit.

It would be interesting to have -- either within trimesh, or within a C++ library that could optionally be shipped with trimesh -- an accessible version of the boolean operation algorithms described in this paper: 
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1605.01760.pdf

I would like to develop this myself at some point, but I'm putting it here to get others' opinions as to the value of choosing this particular algorithm.

The main reasons for thinking it would be good to do this is that CGAL is very slow, and Blender is known to fail with some ill-constructed meshes -- both of which this algorithm seems to address.

Hi! As always, great library, well done.

Question of definitions for you, pertaining to Watertightness of a mesh, as implemented in the `is_watertight` property/function.

In my understanding, watertightness as defined by this func is:
*"if all edges are shared by **exactly 2 faces**, then the mesh is watertight"*

Whereas in some other definitions I've seen, it's:
*"if all edges are shared by **an even number of faces**, then the mesh is watertight"*

Would it make sense to use the latter definition, and thus change the implementation, for cases like a mesh comprised of two cuboids which share a single edge? 

Currently under the former definition, that mesh would be considered not watertight, and returns such as expected from `is_watertight`.

For my use case I'm finding it useful to consider those cuboids that share a single edge as a watertight mesh, so I'm working on writing something which uses the latter definition of watertightness. Just wondering if anyone else might see value in having that available as an option in this lib. If so, what should it be called?

issue_title: Allow session with a device to be recorded
issue_body:
It would be very handy to have netmiko log the session with a device to a specific file. Allowing to keep a log of every device session for logging/auditing/troubleshooting purposes. Like for example keeping all communications of the last month.

Because:

* **Threading/Multiprocessing:** using the logging feature that's implemented now (standard python logging) results in scrambled log files. All threads write at random in the log file. You can't make up what was send to which device.

* **Auditing/review:** For forensics, auditing, logging, troubleshooting... purposes you often need a single file of what happened towards a device. What commands were sent, what was returned. 

* **Current Logging:** With the current logging implementation, it's rather difficult to see at a glance what was sent. You first need to remove a lot of extra information from the logs.

* **Debug log levels:** At the moment you have to enable debugging log levels while I believe this kind of logging should not depend on a debug setting. If you want to do this logging always and in a structured way, you can't do it. Also, by sending this data to the logging subsystem of Python, you risk that it gets send to a remote syslog server. Given the amount of log messages it produces this is not a good idea.

Right now all events report after-the-fact. It should be possible to suppress key, keeping them from being processed by applications down the line. This allows users to create global hotkeys without affecting the focused application, for example.

This is possible in Windows, but would require moving the hotkey processing back into the hook.

Linux seems to be trickier. Maybe relying on X, like https://github.com/alols/xcape ?

Finally, extreme care must be taken to not introduce input lag. Because all hotkeys will be processed in the main thread, blocking the event they analyze, it would be too easy to add precious milliseconds to every key press, which is not acceptable.

issue_title: 32/64 bit confusion on Windows in Process.cmdline and Process.cwd
issue_body:
While considering to restructure my Process.environ implementation I came across the following problem:  In Process.cmdline and Process.cwd ReadProcessMemory is called to get at the RTL_USER_PROCESS_PARAMETERS structure that has the necessary information.  The pointer to the environment variables is also located at the end of this structure.  There are five combinations that have to be considered:  the Python process can be a native 32bit (on a 32bit host OS), a WoW64 32bit process process or 64bit process and the target process can be 32bit or 64bit.

  | Process 32 bit | Process 64 bit
----|----|----
**Python 32 bit (native)** | works | impossible
**Python 32 bit (WoW64)** | works | broken
**Python 64 bit** | broken | works

Currently only the straight cases 32->32 and 64->64 seem to work correctly.  I am currently looking into fixing this but it doesn't seem straightforward and will make the current code even more complex.  Please consider dropping support for these function at least on Wow64.  This would leave three relatively straightforward cases.  In the long run 32 bit native should probably dropped as well.
[0001-test-ReadProcessMemory-functions.txt](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/files/103277/0001-test-ReadProcessMemory-functions.txt)
if someone else volunteers to do this just ask me about it :)

but the docs say that the output should be `Delorean(datetime=datetime.datetime(2013, 5, 6), timezone='UTC')` (which is the correct thing to do according to ISO 8601).

issue_title: How can I make requests in response to updates?
issue_body:
Hello,
as per telegram faq , you can [make request in response to updates](https://core.telegram.org/bots/faq#how-can-i-make-requests-in-response-to-updates), removing the need for another request. while this may not be optimal for sending messages or other methods due to inability to know that such a request was successful or get its result, but it can help speed up inline queries.
the way i can see it we can implant it in one of these ways:

- create a new function for each method that instead of making the request returns the json to be sent as response.
- add a parameter to Bot class `__init__` to chose its behavior between making a post request or returning json
- add a parameter to each function method to define behavior , something like `return_request=False`

i prefer the last one and i can make a pull request for it if you think it is a good addition to telepot,
i'm open to other ideas on how to add this feature though.

Thanks a lot for the quick fix! asn1crypto still cannot correctly parse ASN.1 with nested explicit application tags, e.g. Kerberos AP-REQ. AP-REQ is sequence with explicit application tag 14 that contains another sequence with explicit application tag 1. In Kerberos, all tags are explicit.         tkt-vno      [0] INTEGER,
        realm        [1] Realm,
        sname        [2] PrincipalName,
        enc-part     [3] EncryptedData
    }
    """
    explicit_class = APPLICATION
    explicit_tag = 1
    tag_type = TAG

    _fields = [

issue_title: small feature suggestion - hooks for keyauth
issue_body:
This "feature" would cause the script to grow to about 210 lines, but I think it may be worth it...

acme-tiny is probably the favorite library/client i've encountered so far, because it is so small and concise.  

unfortunately, it can really only be used for file-based authentication which can be an issue if you use roundrobin dns. 

to get around that, my suggestion is to replace the hardcoded file auth with hooks that can be overridden.  i prototyped it here: https://github.com/diafygi/acme-tiny/compare/master...jvanasco:feature-customize?expand=1

I also added a `guid` parameter that can persist an id between the get_crt and authorization handlers.

This would not change how the script is currently invoked, but it would basically turn this into a very re-usable library that would allow people to write small scripts that can save the challenges to databases by customizing how authorizations are processed, or use this as the core certificate signing functionality for larger projects.

issue_title: Don't log with ERROR severity if it's not an error condition
issue_body:
I don't think kafka-python should apply ERROR severity to log messages when reporting problems that would be retried. If a subsequent retry fixes the issue, it's not a problem anymore, which means the original isue should have been reported at "warning" severity, because there seems to be a consensus that warning describes an unusual event that may or may not lead to an error, but is not an error yet in itself: 

- https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html#logging-basic-tutorial
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog#Severity_level
- https://logging.apache.org/log4j/1.2/apidocs/org/apache/log4j/Level.html#WARN

Ideally, an issue that will be retried would be reported as WARNING and as ERROR when retries are exhausted. Although there are borderline situations that might require extra attention, eg. example when consumer polls for messages and something happens, no retries are set up for the failed request, but it would be retried during the next poll.

issue_title: Prefix size is not taken into account for lookups
issue_body:
Hi,
I don't know if it is an expected behaviour, but it does not seem to be when reading the doc.

It seems that the prefix size is not taken into account when doing a lookup:

```python
>>> tree = pytricia.PyTricia()
>>> tree["0.0.0.0/0"] = 1
>>> tree["1.0.0.0/16"] = 2
>>> tree["1.0.0.0/8"]   
2
>>> tree["1.1.0.0/8"]
1
```

I would expect the two last lines to return 1, as an 8 bits prefix cannot match a 16 bits prefix.

Hi,
thanks for making pytricia available as Free Software!

Regarding the license in detail:
 * Wouldn't make sense to have a library like this be available under 
a license with less 'freedom protection'? (The FSF for instance recommends the https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html#apache2 Apache 2.0 license if you want to do this.
Another option would be the GNU Lesser GPL v>=2.1). Usually for a tiny library: less Freedom protection can result in higher usage. This is good for Free Software because for a tiny library people will often not consider changing the license and alternatively instead of using pytricia just pick a different library.
 * Is the current license meant to be GNU GPL v==2 or GNU GPL v>=2? I suggest to add a sentence for this to the README.md. (Usually this is stated with the license file itself and GNU GPL says "or at your option later version" or so. This is good, because otherwise you'll stay incompatible with GNU GPL v>=3 or LGPL v>=3.)

To state my interest: Currently I am helping https://intelmq.org/ and they would rather replace py-radix within pyasn to get the license situation cleared.  

Best Regards

issue_title: Can't enter markdown headings in new posts / comments
issue_body:
Markdown headings are typically expressed [in one of two ways](https://kramdown.gettalong.org/syntax.html#headers): 

```
setext-style h1
===============

setext-style h2
---------------

# atx-style h1

## atx-style h2

### atx-style h3

#### atx-style h4
```

but as noted in the new post / comment helptext,

> Lines starting with '#' will be ignored

Thus, markdown headings below `h2` are removed from the text before posts / comments are submitted.

There is no way to tell the difference between an atx-style `h1` and a comment line, but for the rest, I think a good solution would be to allow lines which begin with multiple hashmarks, followed by any non-whitespace characters. (`h1`s can always be expressed in setext style anyways.)

Here is a regex for matching lines to be ignored (specifically, those which begin with only a single hashmark, or consist of only hashmarks and optional trailing whitespace):

```
/^#([^#]*|#+\s*)$/
```

issue_title: Which year is implied when given just a month and day? Next and last?
issue_body:
There may have already been a discussion about this but I was a bit curious about how the year should be determined when given only a month and a day. This may be an interesting discussion topic for all users even if you're not familiar with the code.

For instance, let's say that today, June 12, 2015 I make a reference to June 10. I'm probably talking about two days ago but parsedatetime assumes I'm looking ahead to 2016.

### Current behavior

Given the source date **June 12, 2015**, parsedatetime will produce the following dates when parsing the text shown in gray:
* `June 11`: June 11, 2016
* `June 12`: June 12, 2015
* `June 13`: June 13, 2015
* `next June 11`: June 11, 2017
* `next June 12`: June 12, 2016
* `next June 13`: June 13, 2016
* `last June 11`: June 11, 2015
* `last June 12`: June 12, 2014
* `last June 13`: June 13, 2014

The logic considers the 365±1 day period beginning today to be my frame of reference. It's not *wrong* since the behavior can be documented and understood easily, but it may not be expected.

### What is expected?

Given the source date **June 12, 2015**, I think that the following would be expected:
* `June 11`: June 11, 2015
* `June 12`: June 12, 2015
* `June 13`: June 13, 2015
* `next June 11`: June 11, 2016
* `next June 12`: June 12, 2016
* `next June 13`: June 13, 2016
* `last June 11`: June 11, 2014
* `last June 12`: June 12, 2014
* `last June 13`: June 13, 2014

This logic considers the current Jan 1–Dec 31 period to be my frame of reference. "Next" means the date is in the upcoming year, "last" means the date is in the preceding year, and the omission of both suggests that the date is in the current year.

#### End of the year

The example above is oversimplified as it considers only today, tomorrow, and yesterday. I don't think it's necessarily as simple as requiring "next" to refer to dates in the following year. If today is December 28 and I want to refer to January 4, I would probably not say *next January 4*; it's just a couple days away. 

Any good ideas for how to handle that? For example, if the date is within ±90 days of today then use it regardless of the year, otherwise assume the date is in the current year. I am afraid that crossing over the new year like that would be more error prone and difficult to understand. There is a fine line here between striving for the most likely natural language interpretation and employing logic that is stable and easy to understand.

### Other considerations

* If we agree that the current implementation is unexpected, how much work would it be to change the frame of reference like this?
* Which other phrases would this change affect? For example:
    * `June`: June, 2015 rather than June, 2016
    * `next May`: May, 2016 rather than May, 2017
* Would this badly affect backwards compatibility for your particular use case? This would probably require a major version update.

issue_title: pyscreenshot.grab() on Mac, box is additive and not absolute.
issue_body:
On linux, the bounding box is absolute, i.e., bounding box `(5, 5, 10, 10)`, it means: point `(5,5)` and point (10,10). However, on Mac, the bounding box is additive, i.e., it means point  `(5,5)` and point `(15, 15)`.  Ugly fix could be:

```python
    def grab_to_file(self, filename, bbox=None, dpi=72):
        # FIXME: Should query dpi from somewhere, e.g for retina displays
        region = self.CG.CGRectMake(bbox[1], bbox[2], bbox[3]-bbox[1], bbox[4]-bbox[2]) if bbox else self.CG.CGRectInfinite

        # Create screenshot as CGImage
        image = self.CG.CGWindowListCreateImage(region,
                                                self.CG.kCGWindowListOptionOnScreenOnly, self.CG.kCGNullWindowID,
self.CG.kCGWindowImageDefault)
```

issue_title: Using callback on a choicebox to update list of choices
issue_body:
Hi all,

I apologise in advance if this shouldn't be posted here, but this is either a quick fix or a suggestion if isn't possible in its current iteration.

I am aware that you can update the text of certain boxes through a callback by using e.g. box.msg = "New Message", when a callback calls a function with the arg box. 

```
def update(box):
    number = random.randint(1, 10)
    box.msg = str(number)

initial_msg = "This demoes interfacing with a callback \nPush to get a random number between 1 and 10"
initial_choices=["Get me a number", "Choice 2"]
choicebox(
    title="This demoes interfacing with a callback",
    msg=initial_msg,
    choices=initial_choices,
    callback=update)
```

Is there a way of updating the list of choices via a callback, in the same fashion as we can update the msg that appears? E.g. box.choices = ["New","Choices"] 

```
def update(box):
    number = random.randint(1, 10)
    newChoices = ["New","Choices"]
    box.msg = str(number)
    box.choices = newChoices


initial_msg = "This demoes interfacing with a callback \nPush to get a random number between 1 and 10"
initial_choices=["Get me a number", "Choice 2"]
choicebox(
    title="This demoes interfacing with a callback",
    msg=initial_msg,
    choices=initial_choices,
    callback=update)
```

Ultimately I would like to create a chain of choicebox[es] to navigate through various menus and having the box flicker as the user does that isn't as 'pretty' as an updatable list of choices.

issue_title: character(len=*) for non dummy variables to be read in.
issue_body:
When trying to read in a string the compiler naturally doesn't know what the size of said string will be, so naturally the variable is initially given (len=*) so that the shape can be defined later. For non-dummy parameters, however, this fails:
```
ucase.f90:9.28:

  character(len=*) :: string
                            1
Error: Entity with assumed character length at (1) must be a dummy argument or a PARAMETER
gfortran: error: ucase.o: No such file or directory
```
As is explained here, http://stackoverflow.com/questions/25051108/fortran-character-len-only-apply-to-dummy-argument-or-parameter, the compiler can't determine shape of the variable since it isn't being passed in but gets read in after the variable has been set up. We may need to look at another option such as though recommended here, http://stackoverflow.com/questions/14765382/fortran-95-reading-a-character-string-of-unknown-length, in order to get around this.

cutplace seems to bomb out when non-ascii characters are involved in a source file.  IE, if you have an e with a tilda over it, cutplace just bombs out.  Is this as designed or could it be addressed or is there a potential work-around?

All three of these documentation sections mention that `python setup.py develop` is the best way to setup the environment for working on scripts.
- https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/blob/master/tutorials/contributing.md#development-installation
- https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/blob/master/INSTALL.md#installation-from-source
- https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/wiki/mtools-Development-Mode

However, pip has [the "Editable Installs" option](http://pip.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/pip_install.html#editable-installs) that does the same thing.

So instead of

```
cd /path/to/cloned/repo
python setup.py develop
```

You can

```
pip install -e /path/to/cloned/repo
```

I think this approach is a bit more consistent and easy to work with.

issue_title: summary() will sometimes cut off sentences
issue_body:
Using the `sentences` parameter with `wikipedia.summary()` will sometimes lead to sentences being cut off.  For example:
```python
>>> wikipedia.summary('Induced radioactivity', sentences = 3)
```
Will yield the following: ` 'Induced radioactivity occurs when a previously stable material has been made radioactive by exposure to specific radiation. Most radioactivity does not induce other material to become radioactive. This Induced radioactivity was discovered by Irène Curie and F.'`

When it should actually be:

`'Induced radioactivity occurs when a previously stable material has been made radioactive by exposure to specific radiation. Most radioactivity does not induce other material to become radioactive. This Induced radioactivity was discovered by Irène Curie and F. Joliot in 1934.'`

I've only noticed this in summaries of articles containing abbreviated names (period), like 'F. Joliot'.

issue_title: Module name may not be sufficient for Python profiles
issue_body:
Python profiles are sometimes hard to read because all you see is:
- module name
- line number
- function/method name

but you don't see
- package name
- class name

There may be a dozen different modules with the same name in different packages, and don't let me get started about methods called `__init__`.

Now it's hard to do anything about class names if the profiling data doesn't contain that information (at least on Python 2.7; Python 3 introduced `__qualname__` that gives me hope for the eventual bright future, although I haven't actually checked if the profiler records those).

Package names are also difficult, since all we have is filenames.  (If the files still exist you could walk up the tree looking for `__init__.py` files: when these stop, you found the root package.  Except Python 3.3 breaks that heuristic with its implicit namespace packages and anyway, you cannot always rely on having the source code available -- what if the profile was made on a different machine?).

Still, not all hope is lost: the full filename is available, and humans can figure out the package name from that (or look up the class name from filename + line number).  Filenames are long, though, and would not fit in the graph nicely -- but wouldn't it be awesome if they were available via tooltips?

Certain graphviz formats (such as SVG) support tooltips.  I have a patch prepared.

  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/speedtest.py", line 685, in __init__

    root = ET.fromstring(''.encode().join(configxml))
  File "<string>", line 124, in XML
cElementTree.ParseError: not well-formed (invalid token): line 3, column 67

Thank you for your uploading the version 1.0 codes which make me joyful to try and modify the codes.
I increased speed prediction accuracy by the following modifications.

1) Increase upload chunk length
self.config['upload_max'](=50) chunks are selected from chunk list generated by 
for size in  [524288, 1048576, 7340032]:
   for _ in range(0, 33):
        sizes.append(size)
 
(524288 * 32 + 1048576 * 18) * 8 = 285.212672 Mbits without 7340032 chunk.
Modify to,
for _ in range(0, 33):
    for size in  [524288, 1048576, 7340032]:
        sizes.append(size)
((524288 + 1048576 + 7340032) * 16 + 524288 + 1048576) * 8 = 1153.4336 Mbits

2) Increase timeout
Timeout is fixed to self.config['length']['upload'](=10 seconds), is not changed by
 --timeout command options. Download timeout occurs when speed is lower than 3275/10=327Mbits/second, lowering speed decreases download chunks and may lower accuracy.

Set self.config['length']['upload'] to the lowest speed of target systems.

3) Simplify join()
It may be a little improvements, however easy to understand.

3.1) No thread.join(timeout=0.1)
Thread flows are controlled by the following lines:
thread = q.get(True)
while thread.isAlive():
   thread.join(timeout=0.1)
whitch can be replaced　by
thread = q.get(True)
thread.join()

Overheads of q.get(), counted as the longest thread run time divided by 0.1, may be removed.

3.2) No pro_thread.join()
Producer and consumer threads are synchronized by a Queue.
The following lines 
while prod_thread.isAlive():
    prod_thread.join(timeout=0.1)
while cons_thread.isAlive():
    cons_thread.join(timeout=0.1)
are replaced by 
cons_thread.join()

How about the above modifications?

issue_title: Files without code (header) are reported as 0% covered
issue_body:
In coverall, files without any code are listed with a red 0% coverage even though there is no code to cover!

Some header do not have any code, thus are adding lots of noise information in the coveralls report: the beginning of the report is flooded by 0% covered headers.
It has 3 consequences:
 - a negative impression (lots of files uncovered even if they have no code)
 - the need to search for non 0 code line files in the list.
 - code coverage drop (pull request warning) on the removal of all function definition of a header (eg: if a header contains one function definition and a pull request moves it to the .cpp, a red warning will be displayed by coveralls as this header coverage will drops from 100% to 0%).

In c++, header usually have a small (sometime big) amount of code so they can *not* be excluded from coveralls (eg: with `--exclude`). 

Would it be possible to report files without any code as 100% covered ?
Add sleep time between two analyzer processes. It should apply to update checker + batch analyzer.

issue_title: Overzealous conversion of "not x in y" to "x not in y"
issue_body:
At some point, no doubt an automated replacement occurred to standardize on the "x not in y" idiom, but it inadvertently caught the docstring for `worksheet.add_table()`:

        Returns:
            0:  Success.
            -1: supported not in optimisation mode.
            -2: Row or column is out of worksheet bounds.
            -3: Incorrect parameter or option.

Used to be (and should be again):

        Returns:
            0:  Success.
            -1: Not supported in optimisation mode.
            -2: Row or column is out of worksheet bounds.
            -3: Incorrect parameter or option.

Also, in `worksheet._parse_filter_tokens()`:

            if operator != 2 and operator != 5:
                warn("The operator '%s' in expression '%s' "
                     "is valid not in relation to Blanks/NonBlanks'"
                     % (tokens[1], expression))

was

            if operator != 2 and operator != 5:
                warn("The operator '%s' in expression '%s' "
                     "is not valid in relation to Blanks/NonBlanks'"
                     % (tokens[1], expression))

(Somewhat ironically, the test here could be written as `if operator not in (2, 5):`.)

As of FIDE's 2018 Laws (or perhaps earlier), fivefold repetition does not necessarily need to be in alternating moves:

> 9.6 If one or both of the following occur(s) then the game is drawn:
> 9.6.1 the same position has appeared, as in 9.2.2 at least five times.

a bug I found in python-chess, version 0.22.1

the problem is in __init__.py, definition of the Board class. More precisely, in the method _transposition_key  (lines 3136-3141). The problem is that self.promoted should not be part of the transposition key, since by the FIDE law of chess, there is no difference between pieces that have been promoted, and pieces that were there from the beginning. This gives problems when determining whether a draw by threefold repetition can be claimed, or whether the position is a draw by fivefold repetition. To solve the problem, you just need to remove self.promoted from the transposition key. This might give problems with some chess variants where the promoted pieces are relevant, I don't know, I didn't check that part of the source. But for standard chess, it should not be there. In the following, a sample code where the bug really appears.





    import chess
    b = chess.Board()
    b.set_fen("5R2/3P4/8/8/7r/7r/7k/K7 w - - 0 1")
    print(b)
    # in this position, White is about to promote a Pawn to a Rook.
    b.push_san('d8=R')
    # now the new Rook is marked as 'promoted piece', so it is not identical to the other Rook
    fenstring = b.fen()
    # the fen does not remember that the new Rook is a 'promoted piece'. 
    c=chess.Board()
    c.set_fen(fenstring)
    # c has the very same position as b, except that the two Rooks in c are identical, in b they are not.
    print(b == c)
    # this returns False. Very bad sign!
    # now let's try to make a threefold repetition.
    b.push_san('Rg4')
    b.push_san('Rf6')
    b.push_san('Rgh4')
    b.push_san('Rdf8')
    b.push_san('Rg4')
    b.push_san('Rd6')
    b.push_san('Rgh4')
    b.push_san('Rdd8')
    b.push_san('Rg4')
    b.push_san('Rf6')
    b.push_san('Rgh4')
    b.push_san('Rdf8')
    b.push_san('Rg4')
    b.push_san('Rd6')
    b.push_san('Rgh4')
    b.push_san('Rdd8')
    print(b)
    # the same position was repeated three times, according to FIDE rules, it can be claimed as a draw.
    print(b.can_claim_threefold_repetition())
    # but this command returns false! A bug.
    # let's try the very same moves on the board c
    print(c)
    c.push_san('Rg4')
    c.push_san('Rf6')
    c.push_san('Rgh4')
    c.push_san('Rdf8')
    c.push_san('Rg4')
    c.push_san('Rd6')
    c.push_san('Rgh4')
    c.push_san('Rdd8')
    c.push_san('Rg4')
    c.push_san('Rf6')
    c.push_san('Rgh4')
    c.push_san('Rdf8')
    c.push_san('Rg4')
    c.push_san('Rd6')
    c.push_san('Rgh4')
    c.push_san('Rdd8')
    print(c)
    print(c.can_claim_threefold_repetition())
    # now this returns true, here the behavior is correct.

The _getStreamURL_ method in [base.py](https://github.com/pkkid/python-plexapi/blob/master/plexapi/base.py) is hard-coded to return URL with transcoded stream. This means that the only way to access the original video file URL is to start downloading it. I also feel like it's somewhat unintuitive - I'd expect that by default _getStreamURL_ would return untouched video file and transcoded video would be provided by overloaded or separate method.


I don't know my way around Plex API but I did some experimenting with PlexKodiConnect which allows to stream untouched files from Plex. This is the URL it uses for direct playback (domain name, token and client identifier redacted):

https://plex.example.com:443/library/parts/260/1524924117/file.mkv?X-Plex-Platform=Windows&X-Plex-Client-Platform=Windows&Connection=keep-alive&X-Plex-Device-Name=PlexKodiConnect&X-Plex-Provides=client%2Ccontroller%2Cplayer%2Cpubsub-player&X-Plex-Token=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx&X-Plex-Client-Identifier=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx&Accept=%2A%2F%2A&X-Plex-Device=PlexKodiConnect&X-Plex-Product=PlexKodiConnect&Content-Type=application%2Fx-www-form-urlencoded&X-Plex-Version=2.3.8

The relevant PKC methods are _getPlayUrl_ in [playutils.py](https://github.com/croneter/PlexKodiConnect/blob/master/resources/lib/playutils.py) and _transcode_video_path_ in [plex_api.py](https://github.com/croneter/PlexKodiConnect/blob/stable-version/resources/lib/plex_api.py)

Parsed(pathname='path/repo.git', protocols=[], protocol='ssh', href='user@example.com:path/repo.git', resource='example.com', user='user', port=None, name='repo', owner='path')

issue_title: How to set number of bytes to transmit (-n)
issue_body:
Different from getting available bandwidth, I'd like to find how much time it used to transfer specific volume of data.

Is there any way to set parameter as the cli command of '-n'?

Dumping a bitarray directly via marshal does not store the correct format:

  ```python
  >>> from bitarray import bitarray
  >>> b = bitarray('10001101')
  >>> b
  bitarray('10001101')
  >>> import marshal
  >>> s = marshal.dumps(b)
  >>> c = marshal.loads(s)
  >>> c
  '\x8d'
  >>> b # for comparison
  bitarray('10001101')
  ```

A workaround is to dump and load mytes:

  ```python
  >>> s = marshal.dumps(b.tobytes())
  >>> c = bitarray()
  >>> c.frombytes(marshal.loads(s))
  >>> c
  bitarray('10001101')
  >>> b # for comparison
  bitarray('10001101')
  ```
It would be more convenient when marshaling bitarrays stored in lists, dicts, etc if it was not necessary to do this conversion.

issue_title: Support providing a Schema for Extra values
issue_body:
Currently the only options for extra values in a schema is to allow or remove them. It'd be nice to support schemas where you don't know what all the keys are going to be, but you still want to validate the extra values against some rules. Example:

```python
schema = Schema({
    2: 3,
    Extra: Schema({'quality': int})
})

# valid
schema({2: 3, 'foo': {'quality': 5}, 'bar': {'quality': 73})

# invalid
schema({2: 3, 'foo': {'quality': ['I am a banana!']})
```

issue_title: [Question] Add project to `Software that uses krakenex`
issue_body: Would it be ok for you to add [this project](https://github.com/Endogen/Telegram-Kraken-Bot) to the section `Software that uses krakenex` of your readme?

Current issues are now [documented](https://python3-krakenex.readthedocs.io/en/v0.1.4/#krakenex.API).

To "solve" them, can probably "just give up and use [`requests`](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/docs/index.rst)", and offload any network issues as "Not My Department".

Need answers:

- [x] Is having a dependency acceptable? (**Yes. Otherwise, endless loop of NIH.**)
  * So far, `krakenex` has had no dependencies other than the "standard library". `requests` is ubiquitous, though, and many distributions provide it as a system-level package, which handles dependencies _of_ `requests` (`urllib3` and `chardet` on my Arch system, with optional `pysocks`).
  * Building documentation requires `sphinx`, which has `requests` as a dependency.
- [x] Should a "unified" `query()` be introduced? (**No. That can be done independently.**)
  - [x] How should it know if an API method is public or private? (**See issue #40.**)
    * May maintain a list for one of the two, and default to the other one if not in list; or maintain two lists, and refuse to query if in neither list.
    * Currently, the difference is that private methods need to set `API-{Key,Sign}` headers, and include a nonce in request data.
    * It seems that having a nonce is not enforced by Kraken for public queries.
  - [x] What should its return format be? (**JSON, as now.**)
    * Whatever `requests` returns?
    * JSON, as currently output by `query_{private,public}()`?
- [x] Should the return format of `query_{private,public}()` remain the same? (**Yes - for now, to remain backwards-compatible. Trivial - return `request`'s `.json()` where appropriate.**)
  * Maybe yes, if `query()` is not introduced, or to remain backwards-compatible.
  * Maybe no, if to be obsoleted or made private; then `query()` returns JSON, and these two a `requests` object, verbatim.
- [x] Should manual connection handling remain possible, or become hidden? (**Yes. That's the whole point - give users a non-NIH way of customising the connection.**)
  * `requests` handles `Keep-Alive`.
  * Allowing to set `timeout` (in `Connection.__init__()`) may become unnecessary.

Answers may depend on current/planned use cases.

As a result, you should only need to generate a universal wheel and then everyone (on all systems) will get the ability to install sendgrid-django with just the wheel, without having to do any extra work.

issue_title: replace() works poorly with init=False fields and __post_init__ functions
issue_body:
The module-level `replace()` function is defined as taking a dataclass instance and a number of replacement values (called `changes`). A new object is created, passing to its `__init__()` the value of fields from the original object or from `changes`, if they're given.

The question then is: what to do with fields that are `init=False`? Since they're not passed in to `__init__`, there's no way to specify them on the new instance. Currently I have post-create logic which says that for each `init=False` field I set the new object's value to the original object's value.

This works great, except where the `init=False` field is initialized in `__post_init__`. If there is a `__post_init__`, then any value set there is overwritten by my post-create logic. For example:

```
@dataclass
class C:
    i: int
    j: int = field(default=None, init=False)

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.j = 10 * self.i

c = C(1)
print(c)
print(replace(c, i=2))
```
Produces:
```
C(i=1, j=10)
C(i=2, j=10)
```
That is, even though the new object's `j` field is calculated in `__post_init__` to be 20, it's overwritten by the post-create logic which copies the original object's `j` value of 10 over the new object's value.

The only thing I can think of doing here is to change the post-create loop to say that it should only copy the original object's value if the new object doesn't have an attribute set for the`init=False` field. But I'm not sure how I can detect that, since all `init=False` fields must have a default value, so it actually is set on the new object, even if it's never touched by `__post_init__`.

Maybe we could just raise an error if `replace()` is called on any dataclass that has an `init=False` field and an `__post_init__` method, since there's no way to know what the correct logic should be.

So:
1. Do nothing, leave the post-create logic as it is now. I don't like this.
2. Try to figure out if the `init=False` fields have been set in `__post_init__`, and if not copy them from the original. Probably not possible.
3. Leave the current post-create logic, but make it an error to call `replace()` on a dataclass that has any `init=False` field and also has a `__post_init__` function. This is overly restrictive, but at least prevents the problem outlined here.

Any other approach that I'm missing?

parglare currently calls recognizers one by one and in case of multiple match do lexical [disambiguation strategy](http://www.igordejanovic.net/parglare/disambiguation/#lexical-ambiguities) on its own based on the priorities and the length of the match.

In same cases it would be better to register a handler that will receive all possible tokens expected at the current place and let the handler decide which of the tokens is the next in the input.

**Motivation:** there are use-cases where we want to do fuzzy matching of tokens. This approach gives us a chance to decide which token is the best match at the given location in case there are no exact matches. We even get a chance to dynamically decide which token is the right one in case of multiple matches.

This stems from issues I was having [here](https://github.com/alexdebrie/serverless-mxnet-server) in handling multipart/form-data with serverless-wsgi.

I've used the `serverless-apigw-binary` plugin as recommended by the [File Uploads](https://github.com/logandk/serverless-wsgi#file-uploads) section of the Readme. I've isolated my problem to [this line](https://github.com/logandk/serverless-wsgi/blob/master/wsgi.py#L78) in `wsgi.py`. Pasting some code in here for context:

```python
75    body = event[u'body'] or ''
76    if event.get('isBase64Encoded', False):
77        body = base64.b64decode(body)
78    body = to_bytes(wsgi_encoding_dance(body))
```

The body is Base 64 encoded, so it goes through the decoding in line 77.

After the body is base64 encoded but before it goes through the `wsgi_encoding_dance` and `to_bytes` functions, the body is a bytes object that is the proper image. **If I comment out line 78, it works fine.**

I added some logging around line 78, and the bytes object is changed when decoding from bytes to str in the `wsgi_encoding_dance()` and then the reconversion to bytes in `to_bytes`. I logged the length before and after the line, and the body went from a length of 145647 to 218875.

I don't know enough about encoding & decoding to fix this myself, so looking for advice. Would it be possible to skip the WSGI encoding dance & to_bytes conversion if the body is already a `bytes` object? If not, is there some other proxy we can look at to see whether the body needs to go through that conversion?

Let me know if you need any more details 😄

Angular Version: 1.4.9 Smart Table Version: 2.1.7 The angular filter function used in the `pipe` function of `stTableController` does a direct value comparison for filtering results. This is ideal for text based results, but when dealing with timestamps, the filter function will not match "March" up against a timestamp for the March timeframe. The only viable solution that I have been able to produce, is manipulating the data set and inserting a string based representation of the timestamp, as another key on the object. For Example: ``` var obj = { created_at: 1456929654000 created_at_search: '03/02/2016' }; ``` Is there a better solution out there for this concept? It seems extraneous to have to manipulate all timestamps to have an additional property on the object; especially when dealing with large datasets. It's also not applicable to just send the timestamp as a string representation, as for sorting; the numeric representation accurately handles ascending and descending order and the string representation would not be as accurate. Also, it is notable that the server's interpretation of the timezone, may not reflect the client's timezone (if handling the pre-rendered text server side). It would be ideal to have the controller understand the built in angular filters, as their rendered text: ``` {{ ::obj.created_at | date : 'MM/dd/yyyy' }} ```

issue_title: Google+ Auth Backend using Email as Unique ID can be dangerous
issue_body:
In https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/master/social/backends/google.py#L12-L17, the default option is to use the Google+ email as a way to uniquely identify the user. However, the new default scopes only include 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/plus.login'. So Google returns an empty email and (at least in Django), we create a model UserSocialAuth(provider='google-plus', uid=''). Since there is a unique index on those two, every single user going forward is associated with that user.

I do not suggest making USE_UNIQUE_USER_ID=True a default because that would cause backwards compatibility problems with existing installations (unless a migration is provided to pull the user id from extra_data). Instead, I suggest adding 'email' in the default scope.

    with aioresponses() as m:
        m.get('http://example.com', status=200, body='test')
        client = await aiohttp_client(my_web_app_instance)
        response = await client.get('/health')  # Raises connection refused for '127.0.0.1:<random port number>'
```

My current workaround is to call `aioresponses` like:
```python
with aioresponses(passthrough=['http://127.0.0.1:']) as m:
   # ...
```

Which is currently fine for me because the mock is abstracted away in one fixture, I don't have to repeat the `passthrough` for every test.

So maybe this should be documented for the newcomer, or perhaps `aioresponses` should automatically find out the test server's URL and implicitly add it to its internal passthrough list. 

Versions:
aiohttp (3.0.5)
aioresponses (0.4.0)
pytest-aiohttp (0.3.0)

diff -ur python-oauth2-master.orig/oauth2/web/wsgi.py python-oauth2-master/oauth2/web/wsgi.py

             and env["PATH_INFO"] != self.token_uri):

issue_title: Updating a request deletes the newest entry from the request list
issue_body:
Unfortunately, #67 doesn't work correctly after all. This small change to the test illustrates the issue:

```
diff --git a/tests/tests35/test_http_aiohttp.py b/tests/tests35/test_http_aiohttp.py
index 0edc396..2fe1557 100644
--- a/tests/tests35/test_http_aiohttp.py
+++ b/tests/tests35/test_http_aiohttp.py
@@ -29,6 +29,7 @@ class AioHttpEntryTestCase(TestCase):
                     async with session.post(url, data=body * 6) as post_response:
                         assert post_response.status == 201
                         assert await post_response.text() == body * 2
+                        assert Mocket.last_request().method == 'POST'
                         assert Mocket.last_request().body == body * 6

         loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
```

The problem is that once the body data gets collected, the call to can_handle() causes the last request to be removed from the list and the body to be appended to the request before the last:
https://github.com/mindflayer/python-mocket/blob/master/mocket/mockhttp.py#L137

The assertion above just illustrates that Mocket.last_request() is actually the first one (the GET request) and not the POST request that was done afterwards.

issue_title: Last Name 'Ma' treated as a suffix
issue_body:
```{python}
In [1]: HumanName('Andrew Ma')
Out[1]: 
<HumanName : [
	title: '' 
	first: 'Andrew' 
	middle: '' 
	last: 'Ma' 
	suffix: ''
	nickname: ''
]>

In [2]: HumanName('Ma, Andrew')
Out[2]: 
<HumanName : [
	title: '' 
	first: 'Andrew' 
	middle: '' 
	last: '' 
	suffix: 'Ma'
	nickname: ''
]>
```

In line 1 the name is correctly parsed.  However in line 2 (when it is listed "last name, first") the Ma is treated as a suffix and there is no last name.  In my opinion, since there is no last name the parser should assume it's a last name and not a suffix.

Does this package support Redis Cluster? I find that to make atomic operation work with multiple keys, {} brackets would be needed, which makes "lock:resource" as "lock:{resource}" and "lock-signal:resource" as "lock-signal:{resource}".

The Redis Cluster document is as follows:

Redis Cluster supports multiple key operations as long as all the keys involved into a single command execution (or whole transaction, or Lua script execution) all belong to the same hash slot. The user can force multiple keys to be part of the same hash slot by using a concept called hash tags.

Hash tags are documented in the Redis Cluster specification, but the gist is that if there is a substring between {} brackets in a key, only what is inside the string is hashed, so for example this{foo}key and another{foo}key are guaranteed to be in the same hash slot, and can be used together in a command with multiple keys as arguments.

issue_title: Explicit loops significantly faster than array multiplications (even with SIMD)
issue_body:
The code to try to reproduce this is here (with a Makefile to compile and do the benchmarking with perf): https://bitbucket.org/fluiddyn/fluidfft/src/master/bench/pythran_implicit_loop

The python code:

```python
import numpy as np

# pythran export myfunc(float64[][])
# pythran export myfunc(complex128[][])
# pythran export myfunc(float64[][][])
# pythran export myfunc(complex128[][][])

def myfunc(a):
    return (a**2 + a**3 + 2) / 5.


# pythran export myfunc_loops2d(float64[][])
# pythran export myfunc_loops2d(complex128[][])

def myfunc_loops2d(aa):
    n0, n1 = aa.shape
    result = np.empty_like(aa)
    for i0 in range(n0):
        for i1 in range(n1):
            a = aa[i0, i1]
            result[i0, i1] = myfunc(a)
    return result


# pythran export myfunc_loops3d(float64[][][])
# pythran export myfunc_loops3d(complex128[][][])

def myfunc_loops3d(aa):
    n0, n1, n2 = aa.shape
    result = np.empty_like(aa)
    for i0 in range(n0):
        for i1 in range(n1):
            for i2 in range(n2):
                a = aa[i0, i1, i2]
                result[i0, i1, i2] = myfunc(a)
    return result

```

issue_title: Instead of Refresh Tokens use freshness date?
issue_body:
Instead of using multiple kinds of tokens (access & refresh), would it not be easier to just add another claim to the access token that stored the freshness date? This way one would not need as many endpoints to handle the various types of login that you describe in the documentation (and I think it would simplify flask-jwt-extended slightly as well).

So for example, rather than just setting fresh as a True or False value, you could set a timedelta for it and add that onto the current time, similar to the other time values in the JWT payload. The decorators in flask-jwt-extended could check for freshness by examining the fresh date. Doing this would eliminate the need for a separate token.

What do you think? I didn't want to make this suggestion too long for fear of it becoming confusing. But if anything I wrote is unclear please let me know!

issue_title: Markdown does not render as html in template
issue_body:
I am adding a very simple blog functionality to my app, where I need some users to get in the admin, write a markdown-based post in it, then saving it and showing in a view (no comments, no previous-next post, no search, no nothing, just user-created pages). I have replaced the `zinnia/entry_detail.html` with a custom template, which has at some point {{ entry.html_content | safe}} # Expected behavior I have a post with markdown markup, like this: *Lorem Ipsum* is simply dummy text of the printing and typesetting industry when I see the `html_content` in the console it is the corresponding html: In [19]: print(entry.html_content)

Lorem Ipsum is simply dummy text of the printing and typesetting industry.

I expect in a template to render the same. # Actual behavior The text renders without the processed markdown, like this:

*Lorem Ipsum* is simply dummy text of the printing and typesetting industry.

Notice that the `

` tags are correct, I am not setting them by hand (my template does not have `

` surrounding the `html_content`), but the text inside is not processed. Is this expected behavior? # Steps to reproduce the issue 1. Setup a django project and install zinnia. 2. In a local app, create the file `templates/zinnia/entry_detail.html`. 3. In `entry_detail.html` add `{{ entry.html_content | safe }}`. 4. Get the rendered source html. # Specifications - Zinnia version: 0.20 - Django version: 2.0.5 - Python version: 3.5.2 - Operating system: Ubuntu 18.04

./bin/demo syncdb

django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: relation "django_content_type" does not exist

Related to #408

`reset` appears to cause unusual behavior that is hard to replicate. Although the background fails to render, the foreground sprites are unaffected. Perhaps recording actions from human input trying to replicate the error by dying is in order to produce a state where the error can be reproduced is necessary  to debug. It's hard to say if this bug is the result of RAM hacking, or something more serious in the underlying nes-py emulator's PPU.

![screenshot from 2018-07-22 13-29-21](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2184469/43048820-6137136a-8db3-11e8-9cf1-0cda9d4a4800.png)

#### Possible Solutions

-   redesign nes-py as object oriented and completely reset the memory on resets
-   add a save and restore state feature to nes-py to reduce the overhead of resets and reduce the possibility of this bug

issue_title: Unable to traverse sorted and filtered results as expected
issue_body:
I was working on adding jsonpath-rw-ext in gabbi (to use sorts and filters) and during my testing discovered that thigngs weren't working as expected. I want to be able to traverse a sorted or filtered result to then use those results.

So I made a [branch to demo things](https://github.com/sileht/python-jsonpath-rw-ext/compare/master...cdent:demo-bug?expand=1) but I'm not sure what the correct way things out to work.

The goal is to know how to articulate tests like this:

```yaml
    - name: test sorted list
      url: /foobar
      method: POST
      request_headers:
        content-type: application/json
      data:
          - alpha: one
            beta: horse
          - alpha: two
            beta: cow
      response_json_paths:
          $[/alpha].[0].beta: horse
          $[\alpha].[0].beta: cow

    - name: test sorted dict
      url: /foobar
      method: POST
      request_headers:
        content-type: application/json
      data:
          objects:
              - alpha: one
                beta: horse
              - alpha: two
                beta: cow
      response_json_paths:
          $.objects[/alpha].[0].beta: horse
          $.objects[\alpha].[0].beta: cow
```

Having `_iterable.SortedThis.find` be:

```python
    def find(self, datum):
        """Return sorted value of This if list or dict."""
        if isinstance(datum.value, dict) and self.expressions:
            return datum
          
        import sys
        sys.stderr.write('datam: %s\n' % datum)
        sys.stderr.write('exp: %s\n' % self.expressions)
        if isinstance(datum.value, dict) or isinstance(datum.value, list):
            key = (functools.cmp_to_key(self._compare)
                   if self.expressions else None)
            return_value = [jsonpath_rw.DatumInContext(value, context=datum)
                    for value in sorted(datum.value, key=key)]
      
            return_value = [value.value for value in return_value]
            sys.stderr.write('return: %s\n' % return_value)
            return [jsonpath_rw.DatumInContext(return_value)]
        return datum
```

gets desired results but breaks the tests here. Adjusting the tests to try and work with this breaks filter and other extensions.

From what I can tell the basic underlying bug is that the thing being returned from `find()` in the extensions is not properly structured to be used for continued processing. Making it a list containing a datum which itself has the expected values sort of does the right thing but I'm insufficiently familiar with the parse tree (at least in tonight's exploration) to know what to do. Halp!

issue_title: Trouble understanding what I can remove for a stripped down machine to machine conversation.
issue_body:
First of all, thank you for putting all this together, your examples are fantastic and I can see where you can get a LOT of functionality from this package. Now, for my particular needs.

I am new to the whole socketIO world and I am trying to write and interface that can talk between a java-based code on one machine implementing socket.IO and a python based machine on the other end (setting him up as the server and the java guy as client). What I really need is just a reliable way to pass some image/json objects back and forth between these two guys and I don't really have a need to serve them up on a webpage or anything.

Ideally I would use something like websockets directly but the java guy has all his client stuff based in socket.IO so there I am. Now, you mentioned I can just get rid of all the Flask stuff and just use any generic WGSI like eventlet to complete the request. Looking at the code, and different implementations I think I can just get rid of the route stuff but I wasn't sure if that is something the WSGI guy is looking for or just useful to actually serve up a webpage.

Sans Flask I am going to be trying to use python-socketIO with python3.5 on my side (hence the eventlet choice). Any advice on what I can safely ignore or possible a more efficient way to handle this would be great, thanks!

    def __init__ (self):
        super(MyFc, self).__init__()

    def forward(self, is_train, req, in_data, out_data, aut):
        x = in_data[0]
        w = in_data[1]
        b = in_data[2]
        y = mx.nd.batch_dot(x, w.T) + b
        self.assign(out_data[0], req[0], y)

    def backward(self, out_grad, in_data, out_data, in_grad):
        dy = out_grad[0]
        w = in_data[1]
        x = in_data[0]
        b = in_data[2]
        dx = mx.nd.batch_dot(dy, w)
        self.assign(in_grad[0], req[0], dx)
        self.assing(in_grad[1], req[0], mx.nd.batch_dot(dy, x))
        self.assign(in_grad[2], req[0], mx.nd.ones(shape=b.shape))

@mx.operator.register("myFc")
class MyFcProp(mx.operator.CustomOpProp):
    def __init__(self):
        super(MyFcProp, self).__init__(need_top_grad=True)

    def list_arguments(self):
        return ['data', 'w', 'b']

    def list_outputs(self):
        return ['output']

    def infer_shape(self, in_shape):
        data_shape = in_shape[0]
        w_shape = in_shape[1]
        b_shape = in_shape[2]
        output_shape = (data_shape[0], 10)
        return [data_shape, w_shape, b_shape], [output_shape],[]

    def create_operator(self, ctx, shapes, dtypes):
        return MyFc()

def get_symbol(num_classes=10, add_stn=False, perturbation_flag=False, **kwargs):
    data = mx.symbol.Variable('data')
    weight_2 = mx.sym.Variable('custom0_w', shape=(10, 200))
    bias_2 = mx.symbol.Variable('custom0_b', shape=(10, ))
    if add_stn:
        data = mx.sym.SpatialTransformer(data=data, loc=get_loc(data), target_shape = (28,28),
                                         transform_type="affine", sampler_type="bilinear")
    # first conv
    # conv1 = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=data, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=20)
    conv1 = conv_pertub(data=data, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=20, perturbation_flag=False)
    tanh1 = mx.symbol.Activation(data=conv1, act_type="tanh")
    pool1 = mx.symbol.Pooling(data=tanh1, pool_type="max",
                              kernel=(2,2), stride=(2,2))
    # second conv
    conv2 = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=pool1, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=50)
    tanh2 = mx.symbol.Activation(data=conv2, act_type="tanh")
    pool2 = mx.symbol.Pooling(data=tanh2, pool_type="max",
                              kernel=(2,2), stride=(2,2))
    # first fullc
    flatten = mx.symbol.Flatten(data=pool2)
    # fc1 = mx.symbol.Custom(data=flatten, )
    fc1 = fc_pertub(data=flatten, num_hidden=500, perturbation_flag=False)
    tanh3 = mx.symbol.Activation(data=fc1, act_type="tanh")
    # second fullc
    fc2 = mx.symbol.Custom(data=tanh3, op_type="myFc")
    # loss
    lenet = mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=fc2, name='softmax')
    return lenet

num_classes = 10
perturbation_flag = True
batch_size = 5
net = get_symbol(num_classes, add_stn=False, perturbation_flag=perturbation_flag)
train, val = get_mnist_iter(batch_size=batch_size )
print train.provide_label
mod = mx.mod.Module(net, data_names=['data','custom0_w','custom0_b'])
mod.bind(data_shapes=[DataDesc('data', (batch_size ,1,28,28)), DataDesc('custom0_w', (10, 200)), DataDesc('custom0_b',(10,))],
                        label_shapes=train.provide_label)
```

## What have you tried to solve it?

1. `a, b, c = net.infer_shape(data=(5,1,28,28), custom0_w=(10, 200), custom0_b=(10,))` infer_shape operation is ok.

Can anyone show me how to use (bind, init, set learning rate) a new parameterizd layer ? Thanks.

issue_title: !!!GPU performance of Cmake built mxnet is worse than Make built one
issue_body:
I have tested the three cases many times as below:
1. When running train_mnist.py(GPU) on the Cmake built mxnet on Ubuntu, the average epoch time cost is 
0.7s. But the Make built mxnet on Ubuntu takes only 0.42s.

2. When running train_mnist.py(GPU) on the Cmake built mxnet on Windows, the average epoch time cost is 0.822s, even longer than running the script on MKL. The CPU(MKL) mode is about 0.6s.

3. The Windows MKL built mxnet version is better than the Ubuntu MKL built one. Each epoch of the former takes about 0.62s and each epoch of the later takes above 0.8s.
It's very weird. I hope the performance differences can be eliminated. I suggest that mxnet should bring a standard benchmark tool and reference performance index to measure running time such as the 'make runtest' of Caffe.

issue_title: test_loss.test_bce_loss has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

I'm currently working on implmenting  facebook fairseq on mxnet, and find SwapAxis doesn't support kAddTo which is needed when accumlating encoder gradient for each attention layer.  Is that easy to add kAddTo for  SwapAxis?  And  is there any other op that doesn't support kAddTo?  By the way, it is very easy to fall in this trap and diffcult to debug.

issue_title: Missing values in enums accross the whole codebase
issue_body: That's not the first time I'm trying to get a value from an enum, which is listed in the documentation, and found that it's not there. For instance, SEE_DETAILS and WATCH_MORE is supported since https://developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/reference/ad-creative-link-data-call-to-action/v2.10, but it hasn't made it's way to this lib even though we are on v2.12 already. I'd expect to see those in AdCreativeLinkDataCallToAction.Type. Also, there is no Ad.Field.creative_id even though that's returned by the GraphQL endpoint for Ads.

issue_title: Impressions returned as unicode string, not int
issue_body:
In the Insights documentation, the stated return type for the Impressions general field is said to be of type Int: 

![screen shot 2015-06-18 at 2 59 05 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7811805/8243287/9ebc9746-15ca-11e5-9eb3-bd962ffdb913.png)

When I am making a get_insight call on an AdAccount object, however, the Impressions field is returned as a unicode string. Here's an example call:

```
account = AdAccount(account_id)

params = {
    'fields': [Insights.Field.impressions,],
    'time_range': {
        'since': '2015-05-15',
        'until': '2015-05-15',
    },
}

stats = account.get_insights(params = params)

print stats
```

What is returned looks like this, and calling type() on the value for "impressions" returns unicode:

```
[<Insights> {
    "date_start": "2015-05-15",
    "date_stop": "2015-05-15",
    "impressions": "27155"
}]
```

Am I missing something here, or are impressions just incorrectly being returned as a unicode string?

issue_body:

issue_title: Permissions based on groups are inconsistent
issue_body:
When a user has multiple groups and permissions on different levels are assigned to different groups, some permissions get ignored.

Given a user with the following groups: 'group1', 'group2'.
If group1 is assigned AccessContent on the container with AllowSingle, and group2 is assigned AccessContent with Allow on a child node, the user will not be able to access the child node or the container node (randomly it seems).

The following test reproduces the issue:
https://github.com/orus-io/guillotina/commit/ed87c4cd12f233d8fed575dce174da8f3791032a

issue_title: GS can't eat its own dog food.
issue_body:
**situation:**
set up a plone 5.1a2 site;.
create a snapshot in portal_setup.
change the day the week should start with in the control panel "Date and Time Settings"
create a snapshot in portal_setup again and make a diff between those two.

i then safed this changed settings to my registry.xml
```
  <record name="plone.first_weekday" interface="Products.CMFPlone.interfaces.controlpanel.IDateAndTimeSchema" field="first_weekday">
    <field type="plone.registry.field.Choice">
      <description xmlns:ns0="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n" ns0:domain="plone" ns0:translate="help_first_weekday">First day in the week.</description>
      <title xmlns:ns0="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n" ns0:domain="plone" ns0:translate="label_first_weekday">First weekday</title>
      <vocabulary>plone.app.vocabularies.Weekdays</vocabulary>
    </field>
    <value>3</value>
  </record>
```

now i set up another plone site and also install my package with this settings stored inside and get an error:
```
Traceback (innermost last):
  Module ZPublisher.Publish, line 138, in publish
  Module ZPublisher.mapply, line 77, in mapply
  Module ZPublisher.Publish, line 48, in call_object
  Module Products.CMFPlone.browser.admin, line 272, in __call__
  Module Products.CMFPlone.factory, line 168, in addPloneSite
  Module Products.GenericSetup.tool, line 379, in runAllImportStepsFromProfile
   - __traceback_info__: profile-akbild.site:default
  Module Products.GenericSetup.tool, line 1414, in _runImportStepsFromContext
  Module Products.GenericSetup.tool, line 1226, in _doRunImportStep
   - __traceback_info__: plone.app.registry
  Module plone.app.registry.exportimport.handler, line 47, in importRegistry
  Module plone.app.registry.exportimport.handler, line 90, in importDocument
  Module plone.app.registry.exportimport.handler, line 246, in importRecord
   - __traceback_info__: record name: plone.first_weekday
  Module plone.supermodel.utils, line 144, in elementToValue
  Module zope.schema._field, line 322, in fromUnicode
  Module zope.schema._bootstrapfields, line 183, in validate
  Module zope.schema._field, line 338, in _validate
ConstraintNotSatisfied: (u'0', '')
```
0 -> Monday (Europe), but every other number from 0 (monday) to 6 (sunday) generates the same error.

**why is that happening?**
as far as i can tell, the choice-field validates a given value (the one from the xml file) against a given vocabulary, which is a dict of number(keys) and simpleterms (values). see zope.schema._field.Choice._validate() for that. but the value from the xml is a unicode string, not an int. -> so its not in the vocabulary.

**further down the rabbit hole:**
the value is represented as unicode as it is returned from this method before: see plone.supermodel.utils.elementToValue(). this method converts everything thats not an ICollection or an IDict to unicode. big problem, as the vocabulary of an IChoice can hold ints or strings as keys (maybe even more other types i am not thinking of right now*)

**solution(s)**
1. rewrite the vocabulary to have better key/value consistency. or
2. extend the choice field to also handle value_type, as IDict and ICollection does. or
3. change the elementToValue method to handle text as integers if it only consists of digits
4. make all GS setup files JSON instead of XML (no just kidding... but would be awesome)

i will implement the third solution, as i am not able (too heavy) to "fix" the Choice field and add value_types to it or reimplement the whole GS-setup infrastructure.

issue_title: Comments are not preserved through loads() + dumps()
issue_body:
The hls spec permits comments (c.f. https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-16#section-4.1, 3rd paragraph).

Some tools use comments to provide extensions to compatible players (such as brightcove zencoder). Preserving comments in their proper locations would interoperate better with such tools.

Here's an example of a playlist with a brightcove-like comment, and which fails the test_dumps_should_build_same_string test:

```
PLAYLIST_WITH_COMMENT = '''
#EXTM3U
#EXT-X-VERSION:3
#EXT-X-PLAYLIST-TYPE:VOD
#EXT-X-TARGETDURATION:8
#EXTINF:8,
http://media.example.com/entire.ts
#ZEN-TOTAL-DURATION:8
#EXT-X-ENDLIST
'''
```

(This example also fails because VOD and TARGETDURATION get reordered, a separate issue.)

A quick debugging session indicated that pythons `selectors.py` tries to squeeze out a file descriptor from the ZMQ Socket object by calling `fileno()`, but the object does not have this method. I monkey-patched something like this into it:

```python
class Socket(_AsyncIO, _future._AsyncSocket):
    """Socket returning asyncio Futures for send/recv/poll methods."""

    _poller_class = Poller

    def fileno(self): 
        return self.FD
```

which resolved the issue. 

Any idea why this is not working out of the box? Any version mismatches?

issue_title: Handle Tree operations in a separate class
issue_body:
Following @ametaireau 's comment, we should create a `Tree` class, which handles its state internally, so we don't have to expose the tree variable to the user of the object.

``` python
tree = Tree() # This loads the tree. In the constructor, do everything you need.

# Then, directly use functions or arguments of the tree. e.g.

tree.key # or tree.get_key() if it takes some time to get the key.
tree.encrypt()
tree.decrypt()
tree.write()
```

issue_title: SFTP target doesn't allow custom port
issue_body:
The SFTP target ignores the port parameter and changes it to 22.

```
class RemoteFileSystem(luigi.target.FileSystem):

    def __init__(self, host, username=None, password=None, port=21, tls=False, timeout=60, sftp=False):
        self.host = host
        self.username = username
        self.password = password
        self.port = port
        self.tls = tls
        self.timeout = timeout
        self.sftp = sftp

        if self.sftp:
            self.port = 22
```

The last two lines in that change the port to 22 if SFTP is needed.  In my case, I need to use 522.

I'm not creating a pull request because this would be a breaking change to anyone that is using the default port as removing the problematic code would change the default SFTP port from 22 to 21.

Any suggestions?

issue_title: Document prune_on_get_work (maybe add config for server's prune period?)
issue_body:
I have a task that needs to retry its ExternalTasks very frequently (we have worker keep alive on as well as retry external tasks). I spent the day digging through the code trying to figure out why the worker wasn't actually retrying the External Tasks every few seconds as per the wait interval and retry delay settings.

Turns out it's because the scheduler was hardcoded to prune tasks once every 60 seconds and the worker couldn't get new work because the tasks weren't pruned. Turned on prune_on_get_work and everything worked fine.

Also, is there a good reason why the prune period can't be dropped to 1 second?

issue_title: Let the dbserver config listening on localhost too even if IP is set
issue_body:
Currently in `openquake.cfg` we have a single config parameter called `host` for the dbserver: this is used for both the dbserver IP (where to push messages) and as the IP where to listen at. On a single machine it's left to `localhost` which is fine. On a cluster the master node IP must be set instead: on master this has the side effect that dbserver is not available on `localhost` but only on the iface associated to that IP.

My proposal is not to change the `openquake.cfg` (which would mean split `host` into `host` and `bind_address`) but, when host != localhost to bind on the defined IP _and_ localhost.
... and deprecate the old oq-engine. Or find a better idea.
