issue_title: Can't open a file on Windows with topen
issue_body:
_Disclaimer: This wasn't tested, but based on a bug report by @jamiekt  on `datapackage-py`. It might be wrong._

@jamiekt was trying to load a datapackage using `datapackage-py`, but it was failing with the traceback:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\JetBrains\PyCharm Community Edition 5.0.4\helpers\pydev\pydevd.py", line 2411, in <module>
    globals = debugger.run(setup['file'], None, None, is_module)
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\JetBrains\PyCharm Community Edition 5.0.4\helpers\pydev\pydevd.py", line 1802, in run
    launch(file, globals, locals)  # execute the script
  File "C:/Users/Jamie/PycharmProjects/untitled/test.py", line 3, in <module>
    p = datapackage.DataPackage('http://data.okfn.org/data/core/gdp/datapackage.json')
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\datapackage-0.0.1-py2.7.egg\datapackage\datapackage.py", line 57, in __init__
    self._schema = self._load_schema(schema)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\datapackage-0.0.1-py2.7.egg\datapackage\datapackage.py", line 327, in _load_schema
    return datapackage.schema.Schema(schema)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\datapackage-0.0.1-py2.7.egg\datapackage\schema.py", line 35, in __init__
    self._registry = self._load_registry()
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\datapackage-0.0.1-py2.7.egg\datapackage\schema.py", line 71, in _load_registry
    return datapackage.registry.Registry()
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\datapackage-0.0.1-py2.7.egg\datapackage\registry.py", line 44, in __init__
    six.raise_from(RegistryError(e), e)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\six-1.10.0-py2.7.egg\six.py", line 718, in raise_from
    raise value
datapackage.exceptions.RegistryError: Scheme "c" is not supported
```

Following his debugging steps, even though that exception class is `datapackage.exceptions.RegistryError`, it's actually being thrown by `tabulator`. What's happening is that at:

```python
# https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-py/blob/master/datapackage/registry.py#L108-L110
    def _get_registry(self, registry_path_or_url):
        '''dict: Return the registry as dict with profiles keyed by id.'''
        table = tabulator.topen(registry_path_or_url, with_headers=True)
        # ...
```

where `registry_path_or_url` is, by default, the path to the local `registry.csv` cache. In Linux/Mac, it'll look like `/foo/bar/baz/registry.csv`, in Windows, it'll look like `C:\foo\bar\baz\registry.csv`. That goes to `tabulator.topen`, which tries to autodetect the scheme (as none was passed). The scheme detection in [helpers.detect_scheme](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tabulator-py/blob/master/tabulator/helpers.py) uses `urlparse(path).scheme`. Trying that method with the example paths, it returns:

```
>>> urlparse.urlparse('/foo/bar/baz/registry.csv')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
ParseResult(scheme='', netloc='', path='/foo/bar/baz/registry.csv', params='', query='', fragment='')
>>> urlparse.urlparse('C:\foo\bar\baz\registry.csv')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
ParseResult(scheme='c', netloc='', path='\x0coo\x08ar\x08az\registry.csv', params='', query='', fragment='')
```

Note that for `/foo/bar/baz/registry.csv`, the `scheme` is `''` (empty string), while for `C:\foo\bar\baz\registry.csv`, the `scheme` is `'c'`. Going back to `tabulator.topen`, the code for detecting the scheme is `scheme = helpers.detect_scheme(source) or _DEFAULT_SCHEME`. So, with a path as in Linux, it'll be `scheme = '' or _DEFAULT_SCHEME`, which ends up being `_DEFAULT_SCHEME` (file). But for a path in Windows, that line will become `scheme = 'c' or _DEFAULT_SCHEME`, so `scheme` ends up being `'c'`. As there's no scheme named `'c'`, tabulator raises an exception.

As far as I can see, that's the source of the error @jamiekt is seeing.

issue_title: How to transfer tokens from non-`contract.eth.accounts` address?

issue_body:
* Version: 4

### What was wrong?

The order of the middleware as passed in to Web3, and treated as part of `add` or `insert`, and returned from `list(w3.middleware_stack)` is inconsistent:

1. in `add` (really, `append`), and `insert()`, the beginning is the bottom of the stack, and the end is the top of the stack
2. in `Web3(..., middlewares=[...])` and `list(w3.middleware_stack)`, the beginning is the top of the stack, and the end is the bottom of the stack

(where the bottom of the stack is the part closest to the node and the top of the stack is the part closest to the Web3 invocation)

This is confusing -- it even caused a bug in #648 .

### How can it be fixed?

I think the second order is more natural as part of the implementation, and the first order is more natural in semantic understanding (where you usually want to add new middlewares to the top of the stack, so when you append/add it to the end of the list, that should be the top of the stack).

Proposal: flip the order in `Web3(middlewares)` and `list(w3.middleware_stack)`.

issue_title: conda "update" and "install" commands can leave an environment with inconsistent dependencies
issue_body:
There have been a number of issues reporting various kinds of behavior which I think all has the same root cause.  The dependency resolution when running "conda install" and "conda update" does not appear to take the other packages in the environment into question.  This can result quite easily in an environment being updated to a state where the package dependencies are not actually met.

For example, let's suppose I create an environment with pandas and h5py, but with NumPy 1.9 initially (let's imagine NumPy 1.10 hasn't been released yet)
```
Rover:~ stan$ conda create -n dep_test python=3.4 h5py pandas numpy=1.9
Fetching package metadata: ....
Solving package specifications: ...................
Package plan for installation in environment /Users/stan/anaconda/envs/dep_test:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    h5py:            2.5.0-np19py34_3
    hdf5:            1.8.15.1-2
    numpy:           1.9.3-py34_0
    openssl:         1.0.2e-0
    pandas:          0.16.2-np19py34_0
    pip:             7.1.2-py34_0
    python:          3.4.4-0
    python-dateutil: 2.4.2-py34_0
    pytz:            2015.7-py34_0
    readline:        6.2-2
    setuptools:      19.2-py34_0
    six:             1.10.0-py34_0
    sqlite:          3.9.2-0
    tk:              8.5.18-0
    wheel:           0.26.0-py34_1
    xz:              5.0.5-0
    zlib:            1.2.8-0

Proceed ([y]/n)? y

Linking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|###################################################| 100%
#
# To activate this environment, use:
# $ source activate dep_test
#
# To deactivate this environment, use:
# $ source deactivate
#
```
Everything is fine and consistent.

Now, some time in the future, let's suppose I try to upgrade pandas:
```
Rover:~ stan$ conda update -n dep_test pandas
Fetching package metadata: ....
Solving package specifications: ..................
Package plan for installation in environment /Users/stan/anaconda/envs/dep_test:

The following packages will be UPDATED:

    numpy:  1.9.3-py34_0      --> 1.10.2-py34_0
    pandas: 0.16.2-np19py34_0 --> 0.17.1-np110py34_0

Proceed ([y]/n)? y

Unlinking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|###################################################| 100%
Linking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|###################################################| 100%
```

Now I have an h5py package that requires NumPy 1.9 installed at the same time as a pandas package that requires NumPy 1.10:
```
Rover:~ stan$ conda list -n dep_test pandas
# packages in environment at /Users/stan/anaconda/envs/dep_test:
#
pandas                    0.17.1              np110py34_0
Rover:~ stan$ conda list -n dep_test h5py
# packages in environment at /Users/stan/anaconda/envs/dep_test:
#
h5py                      2.5.0                np19py34_3
```

I've seen this behavior in a bunch of different (not specifically NumPy) related situations, leading me to conclude that (for speed reasons?) the dependency solver is not considering *all* of the already-installed packages when creating an install plan for an `upgrade` operation, only those in the dependency tree rooted in the packages explicitly listed on the command line.

(This issue probably duplicates other issues, but I haven't seen one that explains the problem generically.)

issue_body:
@epruesse commented on [Tue Apr 18 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1935)

Conda currently (AFAIK) only implements simple dependencies where a package requires a series of packages with an optional range of permissible versions.

There are, however, often more complex requirements:
 - alternative dependencies, e.g. `libxml2 2.1.2 | libxml3* | libxml-ng`
 - exclusions: `pysam >0.8 !0.9.1`
 - other package connections, such as `conflicts`, `breaks`, `suggests`, `recommends`, `provides` or `enhances` (examples taken from dpkg).

Are there any plans to extend conda in this direction?



---

@msarahan commented on [Tue Apr 18 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1935#issuecomment-294974603)

As merely a consumer of conda's capabilities in this area, I don't think conda-build is the right place to file this issue.  Do you want me to move it to the conda repository?

---

@epruesse commented on [Tue Apr 18 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1935#issuecomment-294986607)

Yes, I guess conda/conda is the better place. I had looked at #1590 here which somewhat similar. 

---

@msarahan commented on [Tue Apr 18 2017](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1935#issuecomment-294989052)

Yeah, #1590 is related.  Ultimately, conda-build is bound by what conda can do.  It is absolutely not right at all for conda-build to implement its own parsing scheme beyond conda's, unless that scheme is completely specific to building or testing packages (like the jinja2 scheme mentioned in that issue.)

issue_title: Inconsistent htslib version dependencies in docs
issue_body:
There are a bunch of places in the pysam documentation where the htslib version that `pysam 0.13.0` depends on is specified. 

According to:

- the [release notes](https://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/release.html?highlight=htslib) it's `htslib 1.6.0`
- the [main readthedocs page](https://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) it's `htslib 1.3.0`
- the [pypi page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pysam/0.13) it's `htslib 1.4.1`

I can confirm that `pysam 0.13.0` fails to compile with `htslib 1.4.1` if I link it manually against a homebrew install version on my mac:
```fish
set -x HTSLIB_LIBRARY_DIR /usr/local/Cellar/htslib/1.4.1/lib 
set -x HTSLIB_INCLUDE_DIR /usr/local/Cellar/htslib/1.4.1/include
pip install pysam
```

, which fails with
```
    clang -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -Wunreachable-code -fno-common -dynamic -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -isysroot /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX10.13.sdk -I/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX10.13.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks/Tk.framework/Versions/8.5/Headers -Ipysam -I. -I/private/var/folders/py/9zg6845j2gb664dqkw24wn4w0000gn/T/pip-build-atzru6es/pysam/samtools -I/usr/local/Cellar/htslib/1.4.1/include -I/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.4_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/include/python3.6m -c samtools/bam_sort.c.pysam.c -o build/temp.macosx-10.13-x86_64-3.6/samtools/bam_sort.c.pysam.o -Wno-unused -Wno-strict-prototypes -Wno-sign-compare -Wno-error=declaration-after-statement
    samtools/bam_sort.c.pysam.c:45:10: fatal error: 'htslib/hts_os.h' file not found
```

issue_title: Replica action changes replicas to all indices when list is empty.
issue_body:
The replica action has set replicas to 0 for all indices when the filter returns an empty list.

The below action should only set the replica count to 0 for indices that have a creation date older than 14 days.
```
actions:
  1:
    action: replicas
    description: >- 
        Set the number of replicas per shard for selected
        indices to 0
    options:
      count: 0
      wait_for_completion: True
      max_wait: 600
      wait_interval: 10
      ignore_empty_list: True
      continue_if_exception: True
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: prefix
      value: filebeat-
    - filtertype: age
      source: creation_date
      direction: older
      unit: days
      unit_count: 14
```

Upon running this action it looks to have set the replica count to 0 for an empty list.

issue_title: resetting simulation state is extremely slow, consider snapshotting without shutting down?
issue_body:
All the examples seem to completely restart the pybullet simulations and re-load from disk, such as during a call to `resetSimulation()` which unfortunately can consume the bulk of the time for each training run when executing simple tasks. 

Could you consider adding a `stateid = getSimulationSnapshot()` function that will store state and a `setSimulationSnapshot(stateid)` which will restore state? 

This could assume the objects present in the world do not change, they just return to their snapshotted configuration/position/orientation/velocity/joint angles etc. This could substantially accelerate the training process. Thanks for your consideration!

issue_title: TCPSite name property returns incorrect URL when ephemeral ports are used
issue_body:
## Long story short

The ``aiohttp.web.TCPSite`` object provides a ``name`` property allowing a developer to access the URL provided by the site. When using ephemeral ports the URL returned is wrong.

### Long story

After upgrading to aiohttp 3.2.1 I observed the following deprecation warnings:
```
DeprecationWarning: Application.make_handler(...) is deprecated, use AppRunner API instead
```
I investigated AppRunner API and modified my code accordingly to upgrade it. I then observed errors in unit tests that were related to the requesting aiohttp to use ephemeral ports for service endpoints.

In my Python async applications one or more aiohttp servers are running within an application to provide services such as application metrics, diagnostics, etc. Many applications run on a node (no containers here). Ephemeral ports are used to avoid port conflicts between application services and a service discovery mechanism is used to help resolve ephemeral services. I also use ephemeral ports in unit tests to avoid port conflicts with system service ports and other CI jobs running in parallel.

After starting aiohttp web services within my application I fetch their actual URL and register it with a service discovery mechanism. The URL returned from aiohttp is wrong.

## Expected behaviour

Accessing the ``name`` property of a started ``TCPSite`` object returns the correct URL string.

## Actual behaviour

Accessing the ``name`` property of a started ``TCPSite`` object returns the incorrect port in the URL string when ephemeral ports are used.

## Steps to reproduce

Starting a TCPSite with an ephemeral port (port=0) can be used to reproduce the problem. The following script demonstrates this.
``` python
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web
from yarl import URL


async def demo():
    addr = "127.0.0.1"
    port = 0
    ssl = None

    # Start web site.
    myapp = aiohttp.web.Application()
    runner = aiohttp.web.AppRunner(myapp)
    await runner.setup()

    site = aiohttp.web.TCPSite(runner, addr, port, ssl_context=ssl)
    await site.start()

    # Service is now operational - fetch url to it can be registered with service discovery.

    # fetch URL using aiohttp API call
    site_url_api = site.name

    # fetch URL using my work-around that gets the actual port in use.
    # NOTE: getsockname => IPv4 returns 2-tuple, IPv6 returns 4-tuple
    actual_host, actual_port, *_ = site._server.sockets[0].getsockname()
    site_url_workaround = str(
        URL.build(
            scheme='https' if ssl is not None else 'http',
            host=actual_host if ":" not in actual_host else "[{}]",  # handle IPv4 and IPv6
            port=actual_port))

    # Check if aiohttp returns the correct URL
    if site_url_api != site_url_workaround:
        print(f'ERROR: aiohttp url: {site_url_api} != actual url: {site_url_workaround}')

    # Stop web site.
    await site.stop()
    await runner.cleanup()
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    await loop.shutdown_asyncgens()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(demo())

```

When I run the script I observe:
```
ERROR: aiohttp url: http://127.0.0.1 != actual url: http://127.0.0.1:54804
```
## Your environment

Python3.6
aiohttp==3.2.1
Mac OSX
'server'

issue_title: The way certifi is updated seems hard to maintain in production and is possibly a security risk
issue_body:
This is possibly a misunderstanding of mine, so please correct me if I'm wrong.

The CA bundle is updated in certifi simply by pushing a new version of certifi to github and pypi. This has a simple security consequence: Any Python code that uses certifi does not use the latest CA bundle until certifi is explicitly updated. Unlike traditional package updates, *all* (or almost all) certifi updates are by design security updates (and rarely feature updates), and these are pretty frequent: Once or twice a month. For other packages, security updates are pretty infrequent. Thus, security-conscious developers would want to update certifi with every new release.

Sometimes, this is not so simple. For instance, consider a Python web server that uses requests to make outside REST API calls and certifi to secure them. Such servers can be up for months or longer. Updating the certifi package would require the server (at least the Python part of it) to restart, which can be undesirable.

Another complication is caused by pip's `requirements.txt` format. Many Python hosts (for example, Heroku, or Amazon Elastic Beanstalk, or Google App Engine...) take a pip `requirements.txt` file along with your code and set up an environment using this file. The "recommended" way to create this file is to run `pip freeze`. Unfortunately, this command pins down the version of packages, so you could get something like `certifi==2016.8.8`, which is somewhat of a security risk - you would never get an up-to-date CA bundle.

Furthermore, even if you do change the `requirements.txt` line to something like `certifi` or `certifi>=2016.8.8`, if your environment is set up using `pip install -r requirements.txt` and it already had an old version of certifi installed, it would not get upgraded. It only gets upgraded if `pip install` explicitly gets the `-U` switch; there is no way to "force an upgrade" through the `requirements.txt` file alone.

I am not sure what the best way to remedy this would be, but I suggest decoupling the certifi version from the bundle version and having certifi try to auto-update the bundle every, say, 24 hours, from a reliable source like mkcert.org (using certificate pinning to enhance security and to avoid having a chicken and egg problem).

```python
from schematics.types import IntType, DateTimeType, StringType
from schematics.types.compound import ModelType

class _Detail(Model):
```

issue_title: Allow permission restrictions on staging/<jobid> directory?
issue_body:
I'm using pulsar to get around the limitation of Galaxy DRMAA as a real user, where the Galaxy datasets need to be world-readable. This is not acceptable for our user base. I have the following solution in use.... 

* Galaxy files dir is 660 galaxy:galaxy - so that galaxy itself can see everything, but no user on the cluster can access anyone else's dataset.

* Pulsar is being used to submit external real-user DRMAA jobs, where the 'copy' file action is used so that the private galaxy files/* are staged and available to the job. Anything not private, such as genome indices is using the 'none' action as is world-readable.

* Galaxy/pulsar always can always access what they need, as relevant files always have group ownership 'galaxy'. 

* The pulsar chown functionality gives neccessary real-user rights when jobs run under SLURM on the cluster.

* Am running galaxy and pulsar under umask 0007 so that dataset files aren't created world-readable. This ensures they aren't world readable when copied into the pulsar job staging dir.

This seems to be working nicely. However, instead of relying on umask it would be nice if it was possible to restrict the permissions on the staging/<jobid> directories, which always seem to be completely open (drwxrwxrwx).

I looked around, but couldn't find any configuration option, or obvious place where the perms are set for these job staging dirs.

Would it be possible to allow these dirs to be created (drwxrwx---) so that access can be restricted without relying on the umask when the staged datasets were originally created by galaxy?

Would be happy to work on this for a PR if I can get a pointer r.e. where the open perms on the staging dirs are set - I've looked but I'm lost.

Is there a way to set dependencies before the setup function? Since this replaces the normal setup, it means that pip requires scikit-build to already be present on the system. I'm trying to turn the instructions for my package from:
```bash
pip install scikit-build cmake
pip install -v my_package
```
into
```bash
pip install -v my_package
```
I could check for skbuild when I import, then use `pip.main` from `import pip`, but that would not respect the standard mechanisms for selecting user vs. system, virtual envs, etc.

PS: I'm working on the GooFit package, preparing it for a Python release in a few weeks.

### Overview

This add-on allows to automatically publish pre-release on GitHub. 

A pre-release is identified by a git tag and is associated with one or multiple packages (python wheels, installers, archives, ...)

Project maintainers are responsible to graduate a "pre-release" into "release".


###  Use cases

#### Automatic upload of python wheels associated with  [ITKPythonPackage](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage) project

##### Steps

Every day a *bot* will:
* update ITK description and package version in  `ITKPythonPackage` (see details below)
* commit the changes to `ITKPythonPackage`. 

Then, each CI services (or dedicated build bots) will:
* checkout `ITKPythonPackage` source
* build the associated wheels
* use `publish_github_prerelease.py` to publish the generated wheels as pre-release

##### `update_python_version.py` script

This script is not (yet) part of `scikit-ci-addons` and will live in the `ITKPythonPackage` project repository and can be executed as a standalone script. For example, it could then be downloaded and executed by the *bot* named  [kwrobot](https://github.com/kwrobot).

**Usage**

```
Usage:
  python update_python_version.py /path/to/last/ITK/source
```

**Steps**

The *bot* will:
* clone the latest `ITK` source, checkout `nightly-master` branch
* clone `ITKPythonPackage`
* download the script
* execute the script
* commit and push the change to `ITKPythonPackage`

**Script description: update_python_version.py**

Given the patch to both an `ITK` source tree and an `ITKPythonPackage` source tree, the script will:
* get the local commit date of the `HEAD` of [ITK@nightly-master](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/tree/nightly-master). (Using a command like `git show -s --format="%cd" --date=local`)
* get `<major>.<minor>.<patch>` (By extracting the value associated with variables `ITK_VERSION_MAJOR`, `ITK_VERSION_MINOR` and `ITK_VERSION_PATCH` using script like [CMakeParser.py](https://github.com/Slicer/Slicer/blob/eefeac9286f48552e8418a11f412b2823a09b407/Utilities/Scripts/SlicerWizard/CMakeParser.py))
* update [CMakeLists.txt](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt) replacing the ITK version associated with `GIT_TAG` CMake variable to match the latest commit associated with [ITK@nightly-master](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/tree/nightly-master)
* update [setup.py](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage/blob/master/setup.py) by replacing version<sup>1</sup> `<major>.<minor>.<patch>.dev<YYYYMMDD>` where `<major>.<minor>.<patch>` corresponds the ITK release version and `<YYYYMMDD>` corresponds to the date of the `ITK@nightly-master` commit

<sup>1</sup> To facilitate the update of the version, it could be added to a separate file like `itkVersion.py` or `itk/version.py`

**example of execution**

On unix:

```
# TODO: Delete old virtual env and create a new one (e.g name itk-nightly-wheel)

pip install scikit-ci-addons
git clone git://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage
cd ITKPythonPackage

platform=$(ci_addons publish_github_release --current-platform) # manylinux1, macosx or win
commit_date=$(ci_addons git --commit_date)

./scripts/dockcross-manylinux-build-wheels.sh && \
ci_addons publish_github_release  \
  --clear-pattern "*$platform*.whl" \
  --keep-pattern "*dev$commit_date*.whl" \
  InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage \
  "dist/*dev$commit_date*$platform*.whl"
```

**Remarks:** Invocation of `ci_addons publish_github_release` should probably be moved into  `./scripts/dockcross-manylinux-build-wheels.sh` and `./scripts/macosx-build-wheels.sh`

On windows:

```
$PYTHON_DIR="C:/Python27-x64"
$env:PATH="$PYTHON_DIR;$PYTHON_DIR/Scripts;$env:PATH"

# TODO: Delete old virtual env and create a new one (e.g name itk-nightly-wheel)

pip install scikit-ci-addons
pip install ninja
git clone git://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage
cd ITKPythonPackage

$platform=(ci_addons publish_github_release --current-platform) # manylinux1, macosx or win
$commit_date=(ci_addons git --commit_date)

# Adapted from http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2251622/conditional-execution-and-in-powershell#7272878
$(python setup.py bdist_wheel | Out-Host;$?) -and `
$(
  ci_addons publish_github_release  `
    --clear-pattern "*$platform*.whl" `
    --keep-pattern "*dev$commit_date*.whl" `
    InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage `
    "dist/*dev$commit_date*$platform*.whl"
| Out-Host;$?)
```

**Remarks:** Invocation of `ci_addons publish_github_release` should probably be moved into  `./scripts/windows-build-wheels.ps1`

issue_body:
**How to reproduce:**
1. Create an organisation with a subproject: gitlab.com/myorg/subgroup/myrepo
2. Run tests and upload using `codecov`

**Error message**
==> Detecting CI provider
    Gitlab CI Detected
==> Preparing upload
==> Processing gcov (disable by -X gcov)
    Executing gcov (find /builds/myorg/subgroup/myrepo -not -path './bower_components/**' -not -path './node_modules/**' -not -path './vendor/**' -type f -name '*.gcno'  -exec gcov -pb  {} +)
==> Collecting reports
    Generating coverage xml reports for Python
    + /builds/myorg/subgroup/myrepo/coverage.xml bytes=100016
==> Uploading
    .url https://codecov.io
    .query service=gitlab&package=py2.0.15&token=<secret>&build=63840040&branch=test%2Fcoverage&commit=57dd41a7d05fc84f1a039231575071eb8effa565&slug=myorg%2Fsubgroup%2Fmyrepo
    Pinging Codecov...
Error: HTTP 400
slug must match pattern ^[\w\-\.]{1,255}\/[\w\-\.]{1,255}$

**Workaround:**
add the following snippet in the CI script
```
CI_PROJECT_BASEDIR=$(basename ${CI_PROJECT_DIR})
cd ${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/.. && mv ${CI_PROJECT_BASEDIR} .. \
&& cd ../${CI_PROJECT_BASEDIR}
```
**Tentative solution:** 
change regex to 
`^[\w\-\.]{1,255}(?>\/[\w\-\.]{1,255}){1,}$`

issue_title: [travis] Don't fail "deploy latest docs" if there's nothing new to deploy
issue_body: Currently, since both the actual commit job and the tag job run "deploy latest docs", there can be nothing to deploy (since the tag would be the same, even the "which tag did this build off of" file is the same).  We should either not fail when this happens, or not run "deploy latest docs" on tag deploys

issue_title: Is not possible to create custom cultures in .Net Core
issue_body:
Is not possible to create custom cultures in .Net Core

In .Net framework is possible to create custom cultures, e.g en-US-NY (English from New York). using CultureAndRegionInfoBuilder class  as is describe in this example http://www.c-sharpcorner.com/UploadFile/GemingLeader/creatingextending-a-culture/  .

Are there any plans to implement this in .Net Core?

In the project were I am working at the moment we need this to be able to create different localizations for different dialects  of the same language.

issue_title: Add GH_TOKEN env variable when running tasks based on the token used for the project

issue_body:
The client does not properly handle the case when `--redirect` is specified on the command line. The root of the problem is this argument is treated as a binary variable in `cli.py` (`True` or `False`) and a ternary variable in `client.py` (`True`, `False`, or `None`). The resulting behavior is if `--redirect` is not specified on the command line, `client.py` assumes the user does not want a redirect.

The simple fix is to prompt the user on whether or not they want a redirect if `--redirect` is not included on the command line. A better fix is to allow the user to specify whether or not they want a redirect on the command line and prompting them if nothing is specified.

issue_title: Cert file location for domains with multiple SANs
issue_body:
Very minor issue, but when I ran `letsencrypt certonly` with domains like

```ini
domains = example.com, www.example.com, a.example.com, b.example.com, c.example.com
```

I expected that the cert files would go into `/etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com`, but to my surprise they actually went into `/etc/letsencrypt/live/a.example.com`.

For a better location, I'd suggest either using the first domain name specified, or the root domain if all domains are subdomains.

currently, the code checks rather early for running as uid 0 and terminates letsencrypt if it is not running as root.

this code should be put in a function "require_root" (or so) and calls to that should be put at all places where it is clear that it can't continue without root.

see also #200 - that does not require root (in fact, it doesn't require much at all, except being able to modify DNS somehow).

As far as I can tell on https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html, we don't document supported platforms. We should list them. In particular, I believe OS X isn't fully supported at the moment.

After the great refactoring work done by @BibMartin and, we finally have a coherent way to add new features to the map.

I understand at the moment the suggested way to add features to the map, as the documentation suggests, is the following:

```Python
map.add_children(marker),
```

I propose that we change that to:

```Python
marker.add_to(map),
```

to mirror closely Leaflet API (http://leafletjs.com/reference.html#marker).

Also, we should probably change the name from `add_children` to `add_child`, as the method accepts a single element, and not a list of elements. Hope it's not too late for that, before 0.2.0 comes out. :smile:

issue_title: Mapping result of stop insertion not compliant with HGVS recommendation
issue_body:
When mapping a variant that leads to the insertion (delins/ins) of a stop codon, but where the stop is not at the first position of the inserted amino acid sequence, it results in something like:
input: `print(am37.c_to_p(hp.parse_hgvs_variant('NM_080877.2:c.1733_1735delinsTTT')))`
output: `NP_543153.1:p.(Pro578_Gln598del)`

According to the [HGVS recommendations for protein level delins](http://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/protein/variant/delins/) this is not a valid description of the variant (although it correctly describes the biological consequences):

> p.(Pro578_Lys579delinsLeuTer)
>     the predicted change at the protein level resulting from DNA variant c.1733_1735delinsTTT is a deletion of amino acids Pro578 and Lys579 replaced with LeuTer
>     NOTE: the predicted consequence of variant c.1732_1794del is p.(Pro578_Gln598del). Although the proteins resulting from the changes c.1733_1735delinsTTT and c.1732_1794del are identical, their HGVS description is different. Example derives from the SLC34A3 gene, coding DNA reference sequence NM_080877.2)

So I think that the result of the above mentioned mapping should be
`NP_543153.1:p.(Pro578_Lys579delinsLeuTer)` instead of `NP_543153.1:p.(Pro578_Gln598del)`.

As part of my PhD research on code authorship, we calculated the Truck Factor (TF) of some popular GitHub repositories.

As you probably know, the [Truck (or Bus) Factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) designates the minimal number of developers that have to be hit by a truck (or quit) before a project is incapacitated. In our work, we consider that a system is in trouble if more than 50% of its files become orphan (i.e., without a main author).

More details on our work in this preprint: https://peerj.com/preprints/1233

We calculated the TF for **Powerline** and obtained a value of **1**.

The developer responsible for this TF is:

Nikolai Aleksandrovich (ZyX) - author of 97% of the files 

To validate our results, we would like to ask **Powerline** developers the following three brief questions:

(a) Do you agree that the listed developer is the main developer of **Powerline**?

(b) Do you agree that **Powerline** will be in trouble if the listed developer leave the project (e.g., if he wins in the lottery, to be less morbid)?

(c) Does **Powerline** have some characteristics that would attenuate the loss of the listed developer (e.g., detailed documentation)?

Thanks in advance for your collaboration,

Guilherme Avelino
PhD Student
Applied Software Engineering Group (ASERG)
UFMG, Brazil

issue_body: ![screen shot 2016-09-08 at 1 47 42 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6378815/18343030/658a9df4-7566-11e6-9669-c3715b7fece6.png)

The doc says:
> vmprof.enable(fileno, period=0.099, memory=False) - enable writing vmprof data to a file described by a fileno file descriptor. Timeout is in float seconds. The minimal available resolution is 4ms, we’re working on improving that (note the default is 9.9ms).
> https://vmprof.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#module-level-functions

0.099sec = 99ms, not 9.9ms.

And code is (`vmprof/__init__.py`):

```py
# it's not a good idea to use a "round" default sampling period, else we risk

#
# To avoid the problem, we use a period which is "almost" but not exactly
# 1000Hz
DEFAULT_PERIOD = 0.00099
```

0.00099 = 0.99ms ?

issue_title: Documentation states that dump validates data
issue_body:
The documentation states `Schema.dump() also validates the format of its fields and returns a dictionary of errors.` at http://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/quickstart.html#validation

However, it doesn't appear to do any validation:

```python2
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, pprint

class ASchema(Schema):
    x = fields.Int(required=True)

schema = ASchema()
begin = {}
end = schema.dump(begin)
print('begin: {}'.format(begin))
print('end: {}'.format(end))
```

Output:
```
begin: {}
end: MarshalResult(data={u'x': 0}, errors={})
```

Is this expected behavior?  Are there some types of errors that it will catch?

issue_title: Nested schemas requires all validations to pass
issue_body:
If the validation of a single field in a nested schema fails, then values of the entire nested schema are thrown away. Example:

~~~
import marshmallow as ma

class Child(ma. Schema):
    a = ma.fields.Integer()
    b = ma.fields.Integer()

class Parent(ma.Schema):
    x = ma.fields.Integer()
    y = ma.fields.Integer()
    z = ma.fields.Nested(Child)

p = Parent()
~~~

If I call p.load() with a dict that has 'x' set to a valid integer, and 'y' set to None, the returned data has one value, and the error list contains on error message for 'y'

But if I do the same thing with the child instance ('z') and set for example 'a' to None, and 'b' to a valid value, then the entire nested schema ('z') is absent from the data returned by p.load()

I'm trying to use marshmallow for saving settings to disk. I do *not* want the loading method to skip an entire nested object just because one of the values of it are invalid.

Any ideas?

issue_title: Expand docs clarifying the benefits of using `field_for()` rather than typical `fields.Str()`
issue_body:
I'm still trying to understand the benefits of using marshmallow-sqlalchemy above what marshmallow already gives me. 

The obvious one is auto-generation of fields, but for my schemas, most of the fields require additional arguments such as `dump_only` or `required`, so this doesn't add much for me. 

I checked the docs, but couldn't find much. However, I was just reading the code and noticed that a length validator is automatically included when sqlalchemy has a length constraint on the underlying db column. 

That's really clever--and I think it'd be worth mentioning in the docs. Be nice if there was a clear set of 'here's the benefits of this extension over and above vanilla marshmallow' as well as 'here's what using `field_for(column)` provides over and above the typical `fields.str()` or `fields.Integer()`'

Hi,

I am not sure where to report this, if here or to pyserial, but i am sure, that you will know what to do with it...

While playing with `pyserial.aio` and try to build serial server, which waits for USB/UART adapter to be connected, then process incoming data and if the connection will lost, then again wait for adapter. I start with base example there with virtual serial port (created via `socat`) and all seems to work, except situation when i simulate connection lost (exit the `socat`), where i got exception, which i was not able to catch:

```python
Exception in callback SerialTransport._call_connection_lost(SerialExcepti...s on port?)',))
handle: <Handle SerialTransport._call_connection_lost(SerialExcepti...s on port?)',))>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/serial/aio.py", line 346, in _call_connection_lost
    self._serial.flush()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/serial/serialposix.py", line 550, in flush
    termios.tcdrain(self.fd)
termios.error: (5, 'Input/output error')
```
This exception causes, that the `loop` is not closed inside the `connection_lost` method in `Output` class (because it is not called at all), the connection stays open and when the port is created again, it is not more connected to program.

After small investigation in the `/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/serial/aio.py` file (from `pyserial` 3.1.1 on my Funtoo Linux) i found that in the `_call_connection_lost` (the `SerialTransport` class) is called `self._serial.flush()`, where exception occurs (because there is no more this port in system). After i changed the line 345:
```python
self._serial.flush()
```
to ignore this exception (because it is expected):
```python
try:
    self._serial.flush()
except termios.error:
    pass
```
(and appropriate `import termios` of course) all seems to work as expected, i.e. the `connection_lost` method of the `Output` class is called and loop is closed.

I am not Python nor pyserialúasyncio expert, then i am not sure if this is solution or workaround, but i see that similar solution is

A handful of pvlib functions often cause spit out runtime warnings for things like dividing by 0 or comparing the size of a nan value to a float. We should avoid or silence the warnings that we know are ok. They also make the test output a pain to read.

Here's a possibly incomplete list:

- [x] irradiance.perez divide by 0
- [x] irradiance.perez comparison with nan
- [x] clearsky.simplified_solis comparison with nan

I noticed inconsistencies between the pvlib.clearsky.detect_clearsky implementation and the reference it's based on (M. J. Reno and C. W. Hansen, “Identification of periods of clear sky irradiance in time series of GHI measurements,” Renew. Energy, vol. 90, pp. 520–531, 2016.)

The first issue is in the way slopes are calculated.  When calculating the slopes, the function implicitly assumes a data frequency of 1 minute (e.g., `meas_slope = np.diff(measured[H], n=1, axis=0)`).  Including the time differences would provide expected behavior and would reflect the equations from the original reference.  Furthermore, it may improve the performance of using the default parameters on lower frequency data.

I also believe that the calculation of the final features ("maximum difference between changes in GHI and clear sky time series" from the paper) is incorrectly calculated.  The paper shows this feature as the maximum of the absolute element-wise difference between GHI and GHI(cs) slopes for each window (eqn 14).  The implementation appears to calculate the difference between the absolute maximum GHI slope and absolute maximum GHI(cs) slope for a given window.

This isn't really a "bug", but tagging it as such seemed like the best option.  I'd be happy to resubmit the issue report under a different tag if preferred.

issue_title: z3py: weakening PbEq to PbLe decreases the result of maximize()
issue_body:
I am using z3py to solve some N-queens type puzzles on a Stratego board ([source](https://github.com/rhalbersma/zed/blob/master/src/scout_independence.py)). The concrete problem is to place the maximum number of scouts on a Stratego board. This can be done in two equivalent ways: 
   1. at most 1 scout per line segment (row or column)
   2. each scout threatens no other scout

I express the first constraint set using `PbLe` over lists of `is_scout` boolean variables. The second constraint set is expressed using an `And` over a set of `Not(is_scout)` expressions. 

Using **either** constraint set gives the correct answer of a maximum of 14 scouts (using an `Optimize()` solver and the `maximize()` member function). So far so good. **Combining** both constraints, however, gives the wrong answer of 13 scouts if I keep using `PbLe`. However, if I change `PbLe` into `PbEq`, I get the correct answer of 14 scouts again. (See lines 55 and 131/132 of the linked source).

The fact that a **weakening** of a constraint (from `PbEq` to `PbLe`) can **lower** the result of `maximize()` seems like a bug to me, even in the presence of redundant constraints. Or am I doing something wrong?

Environment: Linux Mint 19 (Ubuntu 18.04 based) using `pip3 install z3-solver` (version 4.8.0) using Python 3.6.5

issue_title: get-unsat-assumptions behaving incorrectly when :produce-unsat-cores is present
issue_body:
There seems to be an unintended interaction between `get-unsat-assumptions` and `:produce-unsat-cores`.

Consider this:

```
(set-logic QF_BV)
(set-option :produce-unsat-assumptions true)
; (set-option :produce-unsat-cores true)
(declare-const s0 Bool)
(assert (! false :named |absurd|))
(check-sat-assuming (s0))
(get-unsat-assumptions)
```

Note the commented out line for producing unsat cores, on line 3. Z3 *correctly* produces the following response:

```
unsat
()
```

The subset is empty because the constant `s0` really has nothing to do with the unsatisfiability of the problem. This is perfectly fine.

However, if I uncomment line 3 leaving everything else the same, z3 says:

```
unsat
(absurd)
```

Strictly speaking this is incorrect, since `get-unsat-assumptions` should always return a subset of the literals specified in `check-sat-assumptions` according to the spec:

> (get-unsat-assumptions) returns a subset a1, . . . , an of the assumptions in the most recent
> check-sat-assuming command. These assumptions are such that issuing the command
> (check-sat-assuming (a1 · · · an)) instead would have still produced an unsat response.
> The returned sequence is not required to be minimal.

Clearly, `(absurd)` is not a subset of `(s0)`. So this behavior does not seem to be SMTLib compliant.

It also appears that there must be a named assertion that causes the `unsat` for this behavior to exhibit itself. If I remove the name `absurd` on line 5, the problem goes away as well.

I suppose a simple fix would be to walk through the final output and filter out anything that wasn't in the last call to `check-sat-assuming`, provided that is the only issue here and there are no other unwanted interactions.

issue_title: Optimization with recursive functions yield incorrect result
issue_body:
Thanks @NikolajBjorner Your recent fixes to recursive functions allowed me to make progress. Alas, I'm still seeing bizarre behavior.

This script:

```
(declare-datatypes  () ((Item (mk-item (size Int) (weight Int)))))
(declare-fun items  () (Array Int Item))
(declare-fun picked () (Array Int Bool))

(assert (= (select items 1) (mk-item 4 2)))
(assert (= (select items 2) (mk-item 4 2)))
(assert (= (select items 3) (mk-item 1 4)))
(assert (= (select items 4) (mk-item 5 5)))
(assert (= (select items 5) (mk-item 3 2)))
(assert (= (select items 6) (mk-item 1 9)))

(define-fun-rec computeSize ((lb Int) (ub Int)) Int
   (ite (> lb ub)
        0
        (+ (ite (select picked lb) (size (select items lb)) 0)
           (computeSize (+ lb 1) ub))))

(define-fun-rec computeWeight ((lb Int) (ub Int)) Int
   (ite (> lb ub)
        0
        (+ (ite (select picked lb) (weight (select items lb)) 0)
           (computeWeight (+ lb 1) ub))))

(define-fun totalSize   () Int (computeSize 1 6))
(define-fun totalWeight () Int (computeWeight 1 6))
(assert (< totalSize 10))
(maximize totalWeight)

(check-sat)
(get-value (totalSize))
(get-value (totalWeight))
(eval (select picked 1))
(eval (select picked 2))
(eval (select picked 3))
(eval (select picked 4))
(eval (select picked 5))
(eval (select picked 6))
```

produces:

```
sat
((totalSize 7))
((totalWeight 4))
false
true
false
false
true
false
```

Unfortunately, that is the incorrect answer. There is a solution such that the `totalWeight` is actually `18`, by picking items 3, 4, and 6.

When I code the whole thing without recursive functions, I get the correct answer indeed. In fact, if you remove:

```
(maximize totalWeight)
```

And replace it with:

```
(assert (>= totalWeight 18))
```

that also works as well, producing:

```

((totalSize 7))
((totalWeight 18))
false
false
true
true
false
true
```

So, I'm not sure what to blame here; but seems to be an interaction between optimization and recursively defined functions.

issue_title: "Cannot compare Money with different currencies" error because of lack of `__eq__` method
issue_body:
Django-money check that the currency are the same before most operations:

E.G, L91 classes.py:

        if self.currency == other.currency:
            return Money(
                amount=self.amount + other.amount,
                currency=self.currency)

        raise TypeError('Cannot add or subtract two Money ' +
                        'instances with different currencies.')

But because the Currency doesn't declare any `__eq__` method, the check is implemented by Python this way:

    self.currency == other.currency 

is turned into

    id(self.currency) == id(other.currency)

It works most of the time, as by importing a currency from the same module, you DO use the same object, and therefor it DOES have the same ID:

    from moneyed import USD

But It won't work if you are comparing instances that have been created in one interpreter, and compared in another, like in the case where you use queue managers like Celery.

There is an easy fix: just declare an `__eq__` method giving an universal way of comparing currencies:

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.code == other.code

While waiting for the patch, people that encounter this bug can monkey patch their own moneyed lib dynamically by adding this in THEIR app's `__init__.py`:

    from moneyed import Currency

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.code == other.code

    Currency.__eq__ = __eq__

Hi,
I use https://github.com/jjkester/django-auditlog package in my project and I found, that sometimes `MoneyField` fields are not tracked well (change between old and new state is detected, but only `currency` is saved).

## Investigation

I try to debug what is wrong and found something very internal.

There is function in `auditlog`, which get list of model fields (https://github.com/jjkester/django-auditlog/blob/master/src/auditlog/diff.py#L31). Basically it just call Django's `instance._meta.get_fields()`. Then it make `set` from this list, for example here: https://github.com/jjkester/django-auditlog/blob/master/src/auditlog/diff.py#L80

The problem is, that before `set` I have list like this:

```
[<django.db.models.fields.AutoField: id>, 
<django.db.models.fields.related.ForeignKey: employee>, 
<django.db.models.fields.DateField: date>, 
<djmoney.models.fields.CurrencyField: amount_currency>, 
<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>, 
<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>]
```

When auditlog put this list into `set`, I get just:

```
{<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>, 
<djmoney.models.fields.CurrencyField: amount_currency>, 
<django.db.models.fields.related.ForeignKey: employee>, 
<django.db.models.fields.AutoField: id>, 
<django.db.models.fields.DateField: date>}
```

As you could see, field `<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>` is missing. The problem here is, that `<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>` **return same hash number** as some other field in my model:

```
(Pdb) out[4]
<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>
(Pdb) out[4].__hash__()
264
(Pdb) out[5]
<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>
(Pdb) out[5].__hash__()
264
```

(`out` is my temporary list of fields)

To be sure, I print also hashes of other fields:

```
(Pdb) [(i, i.__hash__()) for i in out]
[(<django.db.models.fields.AutoField: id>, -34), 
(<django.db.models.fields.related.ForeignKey: employee>, 261), 
(<django.db.models.fields.DateField: date>, 262), 
(<djmoney.models.fields.CurrencyField: amount_currency>, 263), 
(<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>, 264), 
(<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>, 264)]
```

So I go deeper in Django internals.

According to https://code.djangoproject.com/wiki/DevModelCreation#InstallingTheAttributes, fields `__init__` call precede `contribute_to_class` (which in case of moneyfield call `add_currency_field` method). So, first all models fields are initialized (and unique `creation_counter` is evaluated) and only then `add_currency_field` is called.

With this knowledge, I think that current implementation of https://github.com/django-money/django-money/blob/master/djmoney/models/fields.py#L363-L375 is wrong. Particulary this two lines:

```
currency_field.creation_counter = self.creation_counter
self.creation_counter += 1
```

You assign `creation_counter` from `MoneyField` class to `CurrencyField` and then increase `self.creation_counter` **to value that is already attached to another field**. This way, two fields have same `creation_counter` which leads to same `__hash__` used in dicts, sets, etc (see https://github.com/django/django/blob/3c447b108ac70757001171f7a4791f493880bf5b/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L473-L474)

## Solution?

```
diff --git a/djmoney/models/fields.py b/djmoney/models/fields.py
index bd315dc..9458fc8 100644
--- a/djmoney/models/fields.py
+++ b/djmoney/models/fields.py
@@ -10,6 +10,7 @@ from django.core.exceptions import ImproperlyConfigured, ValidationError
 from django.db import models
 from django.db.models import F
 from django.db.models.signals import class_prepared
+from django.db.models.fields import Field
 from django.utils import translation

 from djmoney import forms
@@ -309,6 +310,9 @@ class MoneyField(models.DecimalField):

         super(MoneyField, self).__init__(verbose_name, name, max_digits, decimal_places, default=default, **kwargs)

+        self.creation_counter += 1
+        Field.creation_counter += 1
+
     def setup_default(self, default, default_currency, nullable):
         if default is None and not nullable:
             # Backwards compatible fix for non-nullable fields
@@ -369,8 +373,7 @@ class MoneyField(models.DecimalField):
             default=self.default_currency, editable=False,
             choices=self.currency_choices
         )
-        currency_field.creation_counter = self.creation_counter
-        self.creation_counter += 1
+        currency_field.creation_counter = self.creation_counter - 1
         currency_field_name = get_currency_field_name(name)
         cls.add_to_class(currency_field_name, currency_field)
```

I simply move forward `creation_counter` by 2 instead of 1 (first move in `Field.__init__`, second here) and **update global counter** (so next field in model have no conflict with our `MoneyField`. Meaning of this is that I save two values for our field (one for amount, second for currency).

Second modification is in `add_currency_field` method. Instead of accessing next `creation_counter` I use current and previous value (already saved in constructor).

But I am not sure about side effect. For example original `Field` operate also with `auto_creation_order` (https://github.com/django/django/blob/3c447b108ac70757001171f7a4791f493880bf5b/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L171-L176) but I really don't know what does it mean.

Please take a look.

Thanks!

I'm following the docs on ForeignKeyWidget very closely, and am still getting an error when uploading an Excel file. My resource looks like this:

```
class AssignmentResource(resources.ModelResource):
	instruction_type = fields.Field(

	)

	class Meta:

```

`type` is a string field on the `InstructionType` model. When I try to upload a spreadsheet that has string values, I get this error:

What am I doing wrong?

issue_title: [Help]Can someone explain the two args for steam.client.user
issue_body:
http://steam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/steam.client.user.html 
What is the SteamID format and what is meant by steam as the second arg

- [ ] upload rich presense (serialize bin vdf)
- [ ] SteamUser property for rich presence (deserialize bin vdf)
- [ ] requesting user's rich presence

# Facts

- Rich presence messages make use of `routing_app` field in the message header. The `appid` should be used to separate RPs of different games.

- `steamid_broadcast` - list of steamids to which the rich presence will be broadcasted, usually friends who are in the same game

- RPs can contain proto message serialized in proto text format. Dota 2 has some for when the player is in a party or lobby

as showed in #72, zenodo's bibtex entries are saved as `@data` entry types, which are unofficial bibtex entries, thus unsupported by citeproc-py (which we use to parse the bibtex into formatted text).

possible workaround is to change `@data` to `@article` only when parsing the text, but remembering the original version when outputting to bibtex. what do you think @yarikoptic ?

issue_title: Not all the close neighbours are found until search radius is increased
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import numpy as np
from pyresample import geometry
from pyresample import kd_tree
from satpy.resample import get_area_def
import warnings

# Target area, 4x4 pixels
target_def = geometry.AreaDefinition('areaD', 'Europe (3km, HRV, VTC)',
                                     'areaD',
                                     {'a': '6378144.0', 'b': '6356759.0',
                                      'lat_0': '50.00', 'lat_ts': '50.00',
                                      'lon_0': '8.00', 'proj': 'stere'},
                                     4, 4,
                                     [-1370912.72, -909968.64000000001,
                                      1029087.28, 1490031.3600000001])

# Source area
# These coordinates exceed the extent of target area in the north
lons, lats = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-25., 40., num=100),
                         np.linspace(45., 75., num=100))
data = np.ones((100, 100))
source_def = geometry.SwathDefinition(lons=lons, lats=lats)

# 50 km, 500 km and 5000 km search radii
for radius in [50e3, 500e3, 5000e3]:
    # Hide the warning about "more than 32 neighbours" for larger radii
    with warnings.catch_warnings():
        warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
        (input_idxs, output_idxs, idx_ref, dists) = \
            kd_tree.get_neighbour_info(source_def, target_def,
                                       radius, neighbours=32,
                                       nprocs=1)
    print(dists[0, :6])

```
#### Problem description

In some cases, like the north-western location in this target area (pixel location [0, 0]), the search radius needs to be increased to unreasonable levels before the closest neighbours are retrieved. Reproducable with both pykdtree and scipy.spatial kd-tree implementations.

#### Expected Output

The four locations closer than 50 km distance should be found with all the search radii used in the above example.

#### Actual Result, Traceback if applicable

With increasing radius, more close neighbours are found:

```python
[ 22063.30382373  26983.85276005             inf             inf
             inf             inf]
[ 22063.30382373  26983.85276005  50161.67572158  56500.08927782
  56922.78772955  58391.07322077]
[ 22063.30382373  23653.54759765  26983.85276005  28287.49072995
  50161.67572158  50887.5203979 ]
```

#### Versions of Python, package at hand and relevant dependencies

PyResample develop, pyKDTree 1.2.2 (master branch from github). Might cause the failing test in #112

恳请项目组大神抽空看看是我理解偏差还是确实存在bug，谢谢！！！

issue_title: Error: missing keyspace in migration statement
issue_body:
I have a migration file, referencing its table without a keyspace. The migration fails, with the following error message:

```bash
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cassandra_migrate/migrator.py", line 380, in _apply_migration
InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="No keyspace has been specified. USE a keyspace, or explicitly specify keyspace.tablename"
```

cassandra-migrate could issue a USE statement before the first migration, considering the keyspace configured in its configuration file. If we hardcode the schema in every migration we loose the ability to switch between dev and prod keyspaces.

I really appreciate cassandra-migrate, and am trying to establish it at my company. Unfortunately I am not experienced in Python, to submit a pull request, instead of opening the issues.

issue_title: .gangarc being mangled during new version startup
issue_body:
As reported on the lhcb-ganga mailing list.

If a user has defined the following in their .gangarc then the whitespace is removed when the .gangarc is updated with the new .gangarc when a new version of Ganga is written to disk.

```
> #  String contains commands executing before submiting job to queue
> postexecute =
>    env = os.environ
>    jobnumid = env["PBS_JOBID"]
>    os.chdir("/d6/pbstmp/")
>    os.system("rm -rf /d6/pbstmp/%s/" %jobnumid)
> #  String contains commands executing before submiting job to queue
> preexecute =
>    env = os.environ
>    jobnumid = env["PBS_JOBID"]
>    os.system("mkdir /d6/pbstmp/%s/" %jobnumid)
>    os.chdir("/d6/pbstmp/%s/" %jobnumid)
>    os.environ["PATH"]+=":."
```

I suspect the process which creates the new .gangarc is making use of an rstrip somewhere. This isn't correct imo and the config system shouldn't be stripping whitespace from bad configurations. This should be fixed at some point.

issue_title: Investigate the use of ordered dictionary for schema attributes
issue_body:
As mentioned in the developer meeting on 03/12/2015 I've spotted a problem in Core due to initializing objects can potentially require that other schema attributes be correctly set for a parent object.

I'd like to investigate changing the attributes in the Schema to be stored in an ordered dictionary as of 6.1.15.
The default behaviour would be to have an ordered dictionary but to allow for unmodified classes to still use a dictionary style definition of the schema attributes.

This shouldn't be too invasive but I'd rather not introduce this into 6.1.14.

issue_title: Cache data vs fully initialized Job
issue_body:
This is an issue to remind me to look into the use of Cached data on an object from the .idx file vs loading the full object description from the .xml.

Currently this feels like it's extremely difficult to follow where the actual data is being stored and where it's being accessed.
I've made some improvements with the optimisation work for 6.1.14 but this highlights that this needs addressing properly imo.

I've some ideas on how the code could be cleaned up with regards to this but it should probably be done for 6.1.15 or onwards. Basically I want to remove the overhead in trying to access data that isn't there and make it much clearer where and when a job should be fully loaded from disk in future.
This goes hand in hand with improving the SubJobXMLList which unfortunately is still loading ALL subjobs for many tasks due to some parts of the codebase requesting the whole subjob or all subjobs before it knows what it wants to do with the information.

issue_title: Dirac jobs don't transition to completed
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'm opening a 'meta-task' to look into Dirac jobs not reaching the 'completed' state as there have been several reports of Dirac jobs not transitioning between states as intended.

I'm marking this as Dirac as I cannot reproduce this behaviour with the Local backend so suspect that this is a Dirac issue.
(Although I know that in testing I had Dirac jobs reach a 'completed' state so this could potentially be a Core bug in the handling of GangaFiles or another post-processor I'm not certain)

This may or may not be related to some jobs not being transitioned into a completed state from within the Monitoring. I'm assuming that for the moment the job getting stuck in the monitoring is different to Dirac jobs getting stuck in the 'completing' state.

I'll run some test jobs and see if I can produce a mwe which would help track down what is or isn't going on.

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: Add ability to swallow I/O errors
issue_body:
_From @msukmanowsky on October 27, 2015 2:31_

When monitoring streamparse topologies with tools like Sentry, receiving tons of errors like:

```
Received EOF while trying to read stdin from Storm, pipe appears to be broken, exiting.
Error streamparse.storm.component in read_messagestreamparse.ipc
```

Lead to alerting fatigue (we've had 5.9K of these in just one of our production topologies, as @dan-blanchard knows :)). The IPC layer shutting down like this, is usually because of another issue like some other component dying and Storm killing components or rebalancing a topology. In the former case, the actual exception is captured elsewhere already, in the latter, it's a non-error. Either way, the utility of the IPC exception is questionable.

My gut instinct is just swallow these exceptions full stop, but maybe others have use for them. Ideas on how to handle the ability to stop these from being raised?

_Copied from original issue: Parsely/streamparse#198_

Because of the lack of installation documentation, I was stuck at nothing for 3 hrs.

- Retro 0.5.3

issue_title: Performance issues with large data sets
issue_body:
For our use case, we send a few thousand objects to the client. We're currently using a normal JSON API, but are considering using GraphQL instead. However, when returning a few thousand objects, the overhead of resolving values makes it impractical to use. For instance, the example below returns 10000 objects with an ID field, and that takes around ten seconds to run.

Is there a recommended way to improve the performance? The approach I've used successfully so far is to use the existing parser to parse the query, and then generate the response by creating dictionaries directly, which avoids the overhead of resolving/completing on every single value.

```python
import graphene

class UserQuery(graphene.ObjectType):
    id = graphene.Int()

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    users = graphene.Field(UserQuery.List())

    def resolve_users(self, args, info):
        return users

class User(object):
    def __init__(self, id):
        self.id = id

users = [User(index) for index in range(0, 10000)]

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)

print(schema.execute('{ users { id } }').data)
```

```
import graphene
schema = graphene.Schema(middlewares=[AuthorizationMiddleware()])
```

PEP8 says that "middlewares" is unexpected argument and on execution I get:
`TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'middlewares'`

Graphene version:
---
```

```

Also, why is there no mentioning of middlwares in http://docs.graphene-python.org ?

First of all, I would like to say what a great librarie this is. As @mandx mentioned in #614 , it allows me to use python while speeding up frontend development. So Thanks!!

Now, to my question/problem/issue:  I'm just learning graphene, with the goal of (maybe) one day in the near future writing an integration with  MongoEngine, as this is what I need to use for persistence in the project I'm working on. Thing is, to understend a little whats going on behind the curtains, I executed the sqlalchemy flask example (inside VScode) and set up some breakpoints (inside querys, resolvers, etc) but none of them were triggered.

issue_title: AsyncioExecutor stores every future after executing with no automatic clearing
issue_body:
I have one AsyncioExecutor instance reusing per each graphql query. I've found my application leaking a lot of memory, especially while fetching a lot of data. After some debugging I've figured out that AsyncioExecutor has a list of futures that it executes. And this list grows up after every query and there is no clear until we destroy executor or clear this list manually. Also I can't find a usage of that list, so I tried to remove appending operation and now everything is fine. So is there a point to store that futures?

This case was tested using [graphene-peewee-async](https://github.com/insolite/graphene-peewee-async). If needed, I can provide a test case.

[graphql/execution/executors/asyncio.py#L49](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/graphql/execution/executors/asyncio.py#L49)

I've run into a bug with the AsyncIO Executor when trying to run a query from an application where the event is running. 

I think it's because the executor's implemention of `wait_until_complete` uses `run_until_complete` which starts up the event loop and waits. Since the event loop is already running, it threw an exception when I tried to start it a second time.

issue_title: Update salt worker to write an include minion config file
issue_body: Currently, the salt worker parses the default minion config file distributed by the salt installer and modifies it to manage a few settings. This is rather messy. Instead, use salt's minion config "[include](https://docs.saltstack.com/en/latest/ref/configuration/minion.html#include-configuration)" feature to just write a `minion.d/watchmaker.conf` yaml file with the desired settings.

issue_title: Using `block css` will prevent pipelines from working
issue_body:
Our team is currently on an upgrade path from Django 1.8 -> 1.11
On Django `1.10.8` with pipelines `1.6.1` we noticed a strange inconsistent behaviour.
All our stylesheets would be broken for no seemingly reason. The JavaScript would still load fine. There would be no configuration errors or any warnings in the log, even with `SHOW_ERRORS_INLINE=True`
After noticing that some chunks of CSS would be loaded we discovered that when placing CSS in a block called CSS like

```
{% block css %}
        {% stylesheet 'font-awesome' %}
{% endblock %}
```

The stylesheets would be prevented from loading.
We renamed the blocks for convenience and now pipelines is working again as expected.
Checking the docs such an effect is not documented which is why it took forever to discover.
I will check back again once we are on Django 1.11, maybe it is not affecting that version.

issue_title: Fix bad interaction with GcsFileSystem caching
issue_body:
TensorBoard reads log directories using TensorFlow's `tf.gfile` API, which has built-in support for Google Cloud Storage (GCS) via [GcsFileSystem](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/v1.8.0/tensorflow/core/platform/cloud/gcs_file_system.h).  Unfortunately in TF 1.4+ this filesystem added a shared LRU cache that in its default configuration behaves pathologically badly for TensorBoard's read behavior (specifically, for the pattern of interleaved sequential consumption of 3 or more files that may be receiving newly appended data).

This can cause various issues when running TensorBoard against a GCS logdir:
- #158 Excessive network consumption - network usage downloading from GCS to TensorBoard can be much higher than expected due the cache repeatedly re-fetching the same 128MB blocks; note that this can occur even when no new data is being added to the logdir and TensorBoard is not being refreshed.
- #158 Slowness - the extra network fetches may make TensorBoard slower than expected.
- #867 Stalled/incomplete loading - in some cases this issue will also prevent newly appended data from being read from GCS.

This will be fixed in the 1.9 release of TensorBoard **only when used with TensorFlow 1.9+**.  GCS logdir users who can update to TensorFlow 1.9 should do so.

If you cannot use TF 1.9, we recommend avoiding the use of GCS logdirs except from TensorBoard instances running within the same Google Cloud Platform location where [GCS egress traffic is free of charge](https://cloud.google.com/storage/pricing#network-free).  For example, you can run TensorBoard on a GCE instance in the same region as your GCS bucket, and optionally port forward using SSH if you want to continue to access TensorBoard at `localhost:6006`.

issue_title: Feature request: pandoc-style implicit link definition for headings
issue_body: Hotdoc can currently do this: ```markdown ## Some heading blah blah ## Some other heading As I said in [](#some-heading), ... ``` and have it interpreted as "As I said in _Some other heading_, ..." with a hyperlink. This works because it automatically generates `` from the text of the heading itself, and because as a (Hotdoc-specific?) shortcut, omitting the title of a hyperlink makes Hotdoc infer it from the destination. This requires the author of the Markdown document to know how Hotdoc will mangle a heading title into a `string-suitable-for-anchors`, which is not necessarily trivial if it contains punctuation or even markup. Pandoc's spelling for this (which seems to be a commonly available shortcut in other parsers) is: ```markdown ## Some heading blah blah ## Some other heading As I said in [Some heading], ... ``` (or equivalently the same thing with `[Some heading][]`). Effectively, it behaves as though the author had written the first section as: ```markdown ## Some heading blah blah [Some heading]: #some-heading ``` except that if the author writes an explicit link definition `[Some heading]: ...`, it takes precedence over the one generated by this shortcut. This seems very much like the [Markdown philosophy](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/syntax#philosophy): the writer writes nice human-readable text that matches the heading, and Pandoc transforms it into the machine-readable form (the anchor syntax).

issue_title: Depend on setuptools >=16 for consistent exceptions
issue_body:
* safety version: 0.5.1 & master
* Python version: 3.4
* Operating System: Fedora 25

### Description

safety implicitly depends on setuptools, by way of `from setuptools import setup` in the `setup.py`, however `pkg_resources.parse_requirements` was [introduced](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/6d3753cc8d96814a) is only in setuptools 0.6, which is luckily just before [Ubuntu precise python-setuptools 0.6.24](http://packages.ubuntu.com/precise/python-setuptools) and [trusty python-setuptools 3.3](http://packages.ubuntu.com/trusty-updates/python-setuptools), but safety will very likely fail on anything earlier that Ubuntu precise (not a serious problem IMO, but .. worth painting the picture of this implicit requirement).

A more significant problem arises is when trying to catch requirements.txt parser errors, from `read_requirements`, the *ideal* exception to catch varies on the version of pkg_resources that is installed.  In all cases, `ValueError` is acceptable, however, as of setuptools 16 `RequirementParseError` was [introduced](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/cd79379c95051b94a7fa).

Given setuptools 16 is over 1.5 years ago, and safety is about keeping up to date, I suspect that adding setuptools >=16 will be acceptable.

### What I Did

```
try:
    yield from pkg_resources.parse_requirements(line)
except pkg_resources.RequirementParseError:
    pass
```
Result:
```
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'RequirementParseError'
```

issue_title: Unexpected overwriting of data, possible bug

I have run into what looks like a very troubling bug, either in PyFFTW or FFTW.


I will next describe the faulty behavior I am seeing and provide a minimal 
example so that you can reproduce it on your end.


**What I'm seeing**


I have a need to loop over an array of shape (N, M, M), performing *fft2* and
*ifft2* on each (R, C) slice. I must loop rather than broadcast because of
requirements of my use case that are not relevant here. I must be able to do 
this with both Complex128 and Complex64 arrays. Here is the pseudocode of the
loop I'm trying to do:


    A = Array(N, M, M)
    
    for ii, S in enumerate(A):
        S = fft2(S)
        # Do interesting stuff
        S = ifft2(S)
        A[ii, :, :] = S[:, :]


My problem ONLY manifests for *even* values of M and `dtype` `'complex64'` as
far as I've seen in my testing.


Once `A[ii, :, :]` is set with the values from `S`, the [ii, :, :] slice of
`A` should not be modified on subsequent iterations in the loop.


In the case of an **odd** value of `M`, and `dtype` `'complex64'`, I am seeing
every other slice get *overwritten* in the loop *after* it has already been set

I have not yet tried other data types.


Next I will provide the output a test script I ran for values of M ranging
